
## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Tissue–Material Interaction

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Implant Surface] --> B1[Interface Zone]
    B1 --> B2[Near Tissue Microenvironment]
    B2 --> C1[Bioinert Path]
    B2 --> C2[Bioactive Path]
    B2 --> C3[Bioresorbable Path]
    C1 --> D1[Fibrous Capsule Forms]
    C2 --> D2[HCA Layer Nucleates]
    C3 --> D3[Material Dissolves]
    D2 --> E2[Direct Bone Bond]
    D3 --> E3[New Tissue Replacement]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Material Selection Workflow

```mermaid

    S1[Define Clinical Need] --> S2[Identify Functional Demands]
    S2 --> S3[Load Bearing Yes No]

    S6 --> S7[Temporary or Permanent]

    S7 -- Permanent --> S9[Non Degradable Class]
    S8 --> S10[Final Design Validation]
    S9 --> S10
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define biomaterials. List any four classifications of biomaterials with one example each.**

**Model Answer (concise, 3-mark format):**

A **biomaterial** is a natural or synthetic material intended to interface with biological systems to evaluate, treat, augment, or replace any tissue, organ, or function of the body (Williams, 1987 — definition adopted in KTU syllabus).

The four major classifications are:

1. **Metallic biomaterials** — e.g., Ti-6Al-4V (used in hip stems).
2. **Ceramic biomaterials** — e.g., Hydroxyapatite (bone coating).
3. **Polymeric biomaterials** — e.g., UHMWPE (acetabular liner).
4. **Composite biomaterials** — e.g., Carbon fibre–reinforced PEEK (spinal cages).

*(Examiner note: Naming all four families with one correct example each earns full 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between bioinert, bioactive, and bioresorbable materials with one example each.**

**Model Answer (tabular, 3-mark format):**

| Property | Bioinert | Bioactive | Bioresorbable |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tissue Response** | No chemical bond; fibrous capsule forms | Forms chemical bond via HCA layer | Dissolves and is replaced by host tissue |
| **Example** | Alumina (Al₂O₃) | Hydroxyapatite (HA) | β-Tricalcium Phosphate (β-TCP) |
| **Typical Use** | Femoral head ball | Bone implant coating | Bone graft substitute |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Classify biomaterials in detail. Discuss metallic, ceramic, polymeric, and composite biomaterials with suitable examples, properties, and biomedical applications.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Metallic and Ceramic Biomaterials

**Model Solution:**

**Metallic Biomaterials:**
- **Definition:** Metals used for load-bearing implants due to high strength, ductility, and toughness.
- **Examples:** 316L stainless steel, Co-Cr alloys, Ti and Ti-6Al-4V, Nitinol (Ni-Ti).
- **Key Properties:**
  - High tensile strength (**~480–1100 MPa** for Ti-6Al-4V).
  - Moderate elastic modulus (**E ≈ 110 GPa** for Ti; **~210 GPa** for Co-Cr).
  - Fatigue resistance for > 10⁷ loading cycles.
  - Form a passive oxide layer (TiO₂, Cr₂O₃) giving corrosion resistance in physiological saline.
- **Applications:** Hip femoral stems, bone plates, screws, dental implant roots, stents, pacemaker cases.

**Ceramic Biomaterials:**
- **Definition:** Inorganic, non-metallic materials used where hardness, wear resistance, or bioactivity is required.
- **Sub-classification:**
  1. *Bioinert ceramics:* Al₂O₃, ZrO₂ — used for femoral heads and dental crowns.
  2. *Bioactive ceramics:* Hydroxyapatite (Ca₁₀(PO₄)₆(OH)₂), bioactive glass (45S5) — form HCA bond with bone.
  3. *Bioresorbable ceramics:* β-Tricalcium phosphate — dissolves in body, replaced by bone.
- **Key Properties:** Very high hardness (**Al₂O₃ ~ 1750–2000 HV**), high compressive strength, low fracture toughness (**K_IC ~ 3–5 MPa·m^(1/2)**), excellent biocompatibility.
- **Applications:** Femoral head balls, dental restorations, bone graft substitutes, coatings on metallic implants.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Polymeric and Composite Biomaterials

**Model Solution:**

**Polymeric Biomaterials:**
- **Definition:** Long-chain macromolecules used in both load-sharing (UHMWPE) and resorbable applications (PLA).
- **Examples:** Non-degradable (UHMWPE, PMMA, PTFE, silicone), degradable (PLA, PGA, PLGA, PCL), natural (collagen, chitosan, silk).
- **Key Properties:** Low modulus (**UHMWPE ~ 1 GPa**), high flexibility, easy processability, tunable degradation.
- **Applications:** Sutures (PGA), vascular grafts (PTFE/PET), contact lenses, drug delivery, tissue engineering scaffolds.

**Composite Biomaterials:**
- **Definition:** Two or more materials combined to achieve properties unattainable by any single component.
- **Examples:** Carbon-fibre/PEEK (spinal cages), HA/HDPE (acetabular cups), PMMA + BaSO₄ (radiopaque bone cement).
- **Key Properties:** Tunable modulus via **Rule of Mixtures** (E_c = V_f·E_f + V_m·E_m); can be designed to **match bone modulus** (~7–30 GPa), reducing stress shielding.
- **Applications:** Bone-matched implants, reinforced bone cement, dental posts, orthopedic trauma plates.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Explain in detail the various classifications of biomaterials based on (i) biological response and (ii) degradation behavior. Give two examples per category.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Classification Based on Biological Response

**Model Solution:**

This classification (Hench, 1991) describes the **tissue–material interface reaction**:

1. **Bioinert Materials**
   - *Tissue response:* Form a thin, non-adherent fibrous capsule; essentially no chemical reaction with tissue.
   - *Examples:* Alumina (Al₂O₃), Zirconia (ZrO₂), commercially pure Titanium.
   - *Use:* Articulating surfaces of hip prostheses, dental crowns.

2. **Bioactive Materials**
   - *Tissue response:* Form a chemically bonded **hydroxycarbonate apatite (HCA)** layer at the interface, creating a strong bond with bone (osteoconduction).
   - *Examples:* Hydroxyapatite (HA), Bioglass® (45S5), titanium with roughened/HA-coated surface.
   - *Use:* Bone implant coatings, middle-ear prostheses, dental implant surfaces.

3. **Bioresorbable Materials**
   - *Tissue response:* Gradually dissolve in physiological environment and are progressively replaced by host tissue.
   - *Examples:* β-Tricalcium Phosphate (β-TCP), Polylactic acid (PLA), Magnesium alloys.
   - *Use:* Resorbable sutures, bone fillers, temporary stents.

4. **Biotolerant Materials**
   - *Tissue response:* Not rejected, but a thicker fibrous capsule forms; intermediate between bioinert and bioactive.
   - *Examples:* PMMA bone cement, Co-Cr alloys, 316L stainless steel.
   - *Use:* Bone cement in joint arthroplasty, internal fixation plates.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Classification Based on Degradation Behavior

**Model Solution:**

**1. Non-Degradable (Permanent) Biomaterials**
- Remain in the body indefinitely; removal requires surgery.
- *Examples:* Ti-6Al-4V hip stem, Alumina femoral head, UHMWPE liner, PMMA bone cement.
- *Design considerations:* Must exhibit long-term corrosion resistance, fatigue endurance, and biocompatibility for **> 20 years** of service.

**2. Degradable (Temporary / Resorbable) Biomaterials**
- Engineered to degrade at a controlled rate into non-toxic byproducts that are metabolized or excreted.
- *Examples:*
  - *Polymeric:* PLA, PGA, PLGA, PCL — degrade via hydrolysis of ester bonds; byproducts are lactic acid and glycolic acid (natural metabolites).
  - *Ceramic:* β-TCP — dissolves releasing Ca²⁺ and PO₄³⁻ ions.
  - *Metallic:* Mg and Mg-alloys — corrode electrochemically in saline, releasing Mg²⁺ (essential ion).
- *Design considerations:* Degradation rate must **match the rate of new tissue formation**. For bone, this is typically **6–24 months**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing "bioactive" with "bioresorbable":** Bioactive materials (e.g., HA) **do not dissolve**; they remain bonded to bone. Only bioresorbable materials (e.g., β-TCP, PLA) degrade.
> 2. **Stating "polymer = biodegradable":** This is false. **UHMWPE and PTFE are polymers that do NOT degrade in the body.** Always specify the polymer name.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Modulus values must carry **GPa**; strength values must carry **MPa**. Marks are deducted when units are missing.
> 4. **Mixing up Hench's classification:** Some students write "biocompatible" as a category — this is a property, not a classification. The four valid biological-response categories are *bioinert, bioactive, bioresorbable, biotolerant*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Classification of Biomaterials"**

- ✅ Biomaterials are classified along **five axes**: chemistry, biological response, degradation, origin, and application.
- ✅ **Four chemical families:** Metals, Ceramics, Polymers, Composites.
- ✅ **Metals** are used for **load-bearing** implants (Ti, Co-Cr, 316L SS, Nitinol).
- ✅ **Ceramics** are hard, brittle, and biocompatible; sub-classified as **bioinert, bioactive, bioresorbable** (e.g., Al₂O₃, HA, TCP).
- ✅ **Polymers** can be **non-degradable (UHMWPE, PMMA, PTFE)** or **degradable (PLA, PGA, PLGA)**; natural polymers include **collagen, chitosan, silk**.
- ✅ **Composites** combine two or more phases to **tune** properties; **Rule of Mixtures:** E_c = V_f·E_f + V_m·E_m.
- ✅ **Hench's Biological Classification:** **Bioinert, Bioactive, Bioresorbable, Biotolerant**.
- ✅ **Stress shielding** drives the search for **bone-matched low-modulus composites** (E ≈ 7–30 GPa).
- ✅ **Cortical bone benchmark values:** E ≈ **7–30 GPa**, density ≈ **1.8–2.0 g/cm³**, T = **37 °C**, pH ≈ **7.4**.
- ✅ **Key regulatory standards:** ISO 10993 (biocompatibility), ASTM F04 (implant materials).
- ✅ **HA formula:** Ca₁₀(PO₄)₆(OH)₂ — Ca/P ratio = **1.67**.
- ✅ Remember the difference between *biotolerant* (thick fibrous capsule, e.g., PMMA) and *bioinert* (thin fibrous capsule, e.g., Al₂O₃).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🩺 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Metallic Biomaterials — The Backbone of Load-Bearing Implants

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Metallic biomaterials** are a class of bio-inert or bio-tolerant inorganic alloys engineered to provide structural support, load-bearing capacity, and fatigue resistance when implanted inside the human body. They are predominantly used in **orthopedic, dental, cardiovascular, and craniofacial** applications where polymers and ceramics cannot meet the mechanical demands of physiological loading.

In KTU 2024 parlance, these metals are classified as **"Bio-inert Load-Bearing Biomaterials"** — they do not chemically bond with bone (unlike bioactive materials) but rather allow **osseointegration or fibrous tissue fixation** at the interface, depending on surface treatment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 HIGHLIGHT:** Metallic biomaterials are *always* considered the **default answer** for any question asking "Which material is used for load-bearing implants like hip stems, bone plates, and dental crowns?" — they possess the unique combination of **high tensile strength, fracture toughness, ductility, and fabricability** that no other biomaterial family can match.

### 1.2 The "Three Pillars" of Metallic Implant Selection

Before diving into specific alloys, every KTU question on this topic ultimately tests the student's grasp of **three fundamental engineering trade-offs**:

| Pillar | Engineering Question | Clinical Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Biocompatibility** | Will the body reject it or release toxic ions? | Inflammation, toxicity, carcinogenicity |
| **Mechanical Performance** | Can it survive cyclic physiological loads? | Implant fracture, loosening |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | Will it degrade in saline physiological environment (~0.9% NaCl, 37°C, pH 7.4)? | Metal ion release, implant failure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Skyscraper in Salty Water"**
> Imagine building a steel skyscraper and then submerging the lower 30 floors permanently in warm, salty seawater. That is *exactly* what happens to a metallic implant: the human body is essentially a **warm (37°C), aerated, chloride-rich saline bath** (≈ 0.9% NaCl). Any metal that cannot resist chloride-induced **pitting corrosion, crevice corrosion, and stress corrosion cracking** is unfit for long-term implantation. This is why **316L stainless steel, Co-Cr alloys, and Ti alloys** dominate — they form a stable, self-healing **passive oxide layer** (Cr₂O₃ for SS/CoCr, TiO₂ for titanium) that protects them from this hostile environment.

### 1.3 Classification of Metallic Biomaterials (KTU Module 1 Map)

The metals covered in this KTU module are logically classified into **five families**:

1. **Stainless Steels** — Austenitic 316L (the workhorse)
2. **Cobalt-Chromium (Co-Cr) Alloys** — Cast & wrought variants
3. **Titanium-Based Alloys** — Commercially Pure (CP) Ti & Ti-6Al-4V
4. **Dental Metals** — Au alloys, Ag-amalgams, Ni-Cr, base metal alloys
5. **Other/Specialty Metals** — Ni-Ti (Nitinol), Mg alloys, Ta, Zr, Pt

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          CLASSIFICATION OF METALLIC BIOMATERIALS           │
├──────────────┬──────────────┬──────────────┬───────────────┤
│  STAINLESS   │   Co-Cr      │  TITANIUM    │   SPECIALTY   │
│   STEEL      │  ALLOYS      │   ALLOYS     │    METALS     │
│              │              │              │               │
│ • 316L       │ • CoCrMo     │ • CP-Ti      │ • Ni-Ti       │
│ • 304        │ • CoNiCrMo   │ • Ti-6Al-4V  │ • Mg alloys   │
│ • 316        │ • CoCrWNi    │ • Ti-6Al-7Nb │ • Tantalum    │
│              │              │ • Ti-13Nb-13Zr│ • Zirconium  │
└──────────────┴──────────────┴──────────────┴───────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
              ┌──────────────────────────┐
              │   DENTAL METALS          │
              │  • Au-Pd / Au-Pt alloys  │
              │  • Ag-Hg (Amalgam)       │
              │  • Ni-Cr / Co-Cr         │
              │  • Ti (implants)         │
              └──────────────────────────┘
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stainless Steels — The "Austenitic Workhorse"

### 2.1.1 Why 316L and Not 304 or 316?

In KTU examination questions, the **grade "316L"** is the **only acceptable answer** for surgical implant stainless steel. Let us dissect why.

**Composition of 316L (ASTM F138 / F139):**

| Element | wt% | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Fe** | Balance (~65%) | Base metal |
| **Cr** | **17–20%** | Forms passive Cr₂O₃ layer |
| **Ni** | **12–14%** | Stabilises austenitic (FCC) phase |
| **Mo** | **2–4%** | Boosts pitting resistance |
| **C** | **≤ 0.03%** ("L" = Low carbon) | Prevents carbide precipitation |
| **Mn** | ≤ 2% | Deoxidiser, austenite stabiliser |
| **Si, P, S** | Traces | Minor roles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the "L" (Low Carbon ≤ 0.03%)?**
> In standard 316 stainless steel, carbon reacts with chromium at grain boundaries (450–850°C) to form **chromium carbide (Cr₂₃C₆)**. This is called **"sensitisation"**. It depletes Cr from grain boundaries, causing **intergranular corrosion** — a catastrophic failure mode for implants. By reducing C below 0.03%, the carbide cannot form, preserving the protective Cr layer.

### 2.1.2 Mechanical Properties of 316L

| Property | 316L Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Density | **8.0 g/cm³** | Higher than Ti (4.5 g/cm³) → heavier implants |
| Young's Modulus | **~193 GPa** | Far from cortical bone (~17 GPa) → stress shielding |
| Yield Strength (annealed) | **~200 MPa** | |
| UTS | **~550 MPa** | Sufficient for most non-cemented stems |
| Elongation | **~40–50%** | Excellent ductility (cold-formable) |
| Fatigue Endurance Limit | **~240–280 MPa** | At 10⁷ cycles |

### 2.1.3 Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN) — KTU Favourite Formula

$$\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$$

For **316L**: PREN = 17 + 3.3(2.5) + 16(0.1) ≈ **25.7** — adequate but lower than Co-Cr (~30) and Ti (~35).

---

## 2.2 Cobalt-Chromium (Co-Cr) Alloys

These are the **"premium orthopaedic alloys"** used in highly loaded joints like total hip replacements (THR) and knee prostheses.

### 2.2.1 Composition Variants (ASTM F75, F90, F562)

| Alloy (ASTM) | Composition (wt%) | Form | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F75 (CoCrMo)** | Co-28Cr-6Mo | Cast | Femoral stems, dental |
| **F90 (CoCrWNi)** | Co-20Cr-10Ni-15W | Wrought | Fracture plates, stems |
| **F562 (CoNiCrMo)** | Co-35Ni-20Cr-10Mo | Wrought (cold-worked) | Hip stems, heart valves |

### 2.2.2 Why Two Crystallographic Phases Matter

Co-Cr alloys exhibit a **two-phase microstructure**:
- **Matrix: FCC (γ-phase, ductile)** — stable at high temperatures
- **Hard second phase: HCP (ε-phase, brittle)** — forms on cooling

Cold working promotes the **HCP ε-phase**, which dramatically increases strength. This is why wrought Co-Cr (F562) is much stronger than cast Co-Cr (F75).

### 2.2.3 Key Properties

| Property | Cast CoCrMo (F75) | Wrought CoNiCrMo (F562) |
|---|---|---|
| Density | 8.3 g/cm³ | 8.4 g/cm³ |
| Young's Modulus | **~210 GPa** | ~230 GPa |
| Yield Strength | ~450 MPa | **~1000 MPa** (cold worked) |
| UTS | ~700 MPa | **~1300 MPa** |
| Elongation | ~8% | ~10% |
| Fatigue Strength | ~300 MPa | **~600 MPa** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Wear Resistance Advantage:** Co-Cr alloys are routinely used as the **femoral head** in hip implants that articulate against UHMWPE (Ultra-High Molecular Weight Polyethylene) liners. The combination of high hardness (~40 HRC) and self-lubricating passivation makes them the gold standard for wear surfaces.

---

## 2.3 Titanium and Titanium Alloys — The "Biocompatible Champion"

### 2.3.1 The Magic of TiO₂

Pure titanium spontaneously forms a **~4 nm thick TiO₂ passive layer** in the presence of even trace amounts of oxygen. This layer:
- Is **chemically inert** in body fluid
- **Self-heals instantaneously** if scratched (in the presence of O₂/H₂O)
- Promotes **osseointegration** (direct bone bonding)
- Has a **low ionic dissolution rate** → minimal ion release

### 2.3.2 Commercially Pure (CP) Titanium — Four Grades (ASTM F67)

| Grade | O content (max wt%) | Yield Strength | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Grade 1** | 0.18% | 170 MPa | Ductile — plates, mesh |
| **Grade 2** | 0.25% | 280 MPa | Most common — dental implants |
| **Grade 3** | 0.35% | 380 MPa | Higher strength |
| **Grade 4** | 0.40% | 550 MPa | Highest strength CP-Ti |

### 2.3.3 Ti-6Al-4V (ASTM F136) — The α+β Workhorse

This is the **"most implanted titanium alloy in history"**. It contains:
- **6% Aluminium (Al)** → α-phase stabiliser (raises strength)
- **4% Vanadium (V)** → β-phase stabiliser (improves formability)
- **0.25% Fe, 0.2% O** (max)
- **Balance Ti**

**Mechanical Properties:**

| Property | CP-Ti (Gr 4) | Ti-6Al-4V (annealed) |
|---|---|---|
| Density | **4.51 g/cm³** | **4.43 g/cm³** |
| Young's Modulus | ~105 GPa | **~110 GPa** |
| Yield Strength | 550 MPa | **~880 MPa** |
| UTS | 680 MPa | **~950 MPa** |
| Elongation | 15% | 14% |
| Fatigue Strength | ~300 MPa | ~500 MPa |

### 2.3.4 The "Stress Shielding" Problem — A KTU Favourite

**Wolff's Law** states that bone remodels in response to mechanical loading. If an implant carries too much of the load (because it is much stiffer than bone), the surrounding bone **resorbs**, loosening the implant.

$$\sigma_{\text{bone}} \propto \frac{E_{\text{bone}}}{E_{\text{implant}}} \quad \text{(qualitative relationship)}$$

| Material | E (GPa) | E_implant / E_bone | Stress Shielding |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cortical Bone | 17 | 1 | None |
| **Ti-6Al-4V** | 110 | **6.5×** | Moderate |
| Co-Cr | 210 | 12.3× | Severe |
| 316L SS | 193 | 11.3× | Severe |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Advanced Materials:** Newer **β-Ti alloys** like **Ti-13Nb-13Zr** and **Ti-35Nb-5Ta-7Zr (TNZT)** have E ≈ 55–80 GPa, much closer to bone → drastically reduced stress shielding. This is a **frequent KTU question** topic on "next-generation implant materials."

### 2.3.5 Modulus Reduction Strategies (Exam-Oriented)

To lower the effective modulus, modern research uses:
1. **Porous Ti scaffolds** (E controllable from 1 to 100 GPa)
2. **β-phase stabilising alloying** (Nb, Zr, Ta, Mo → retain BCC β-phase at RT)
3. **Composite Ti / HA coatings**

---

## 2.4 Dental Metals — A Specialised Sub-Family

### 2.4.1 The Four Functional Categories

| Category | Example Alloys | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Noble alloys for crowns/bridges** | Au-Pd, Au-Pt, Au-Ag-Pd | Inert, precise marginal fit |
| **Base metal alloys (PFM)** | Ni-Cr, Co-Cr | Porcelain-fused-to-metal substructure |
| **Amalgam** | Ag-Sn-Cu + Hg | Direct dental filling (declining use) |
| **Endodontic / Implant** | CP-Ti, Ti-6Al-4V | Root-form dental implants |

### 2.4.2 The ADA Noble Metal Classification

| Class | Noble Metal Content | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **High noble** | ≥ 60% noble, ≥ 40% Au | Au-Pt, Au-Pd |
| **Noble** | ≥ 25% noble | Pd-Ag, Pd-Cu |
| **Base metal** | < 25% noble | Ni-Cr, Co-Cr |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **"precious metals"** (Au, Pt, Pd, Ag) with **"noble metals"** (Au, Pt, Pd — those resistant to oxidation in air). Silver (Ag) tarnishes → it is precious but **not noble**.

### 2.4.3 Dental Amalgam — Composition

**Composition:** Ag (40–55%) + Sn (25–30%) + Cu (up to 30%) + Zn (1–2%) + Hg (mixing liquid, ~50% of final mix).

The **γ-phase (Ag₃Sn)** reacts with Hg to form **γ₁ (Ag₂Hg₃) + γ₂ (Sn₈Hg)**. Modern "high-copper" amalgams (>20% Cu) eliminate the weak γ₂ phase, improving longevity.

---

## 2.5 Other / Specialty Metals

### 2.5.1 Ni-Ti (Nitinol) — The Shape Memory Alloy

**Composition:** ~55% Ni, 45% Ti (atomic %).

**Unique Phenomena:**

1. **Shape Memory Effect (SME):** Deformed martensite reverts to original austenite shape upon heating above transformation temperature.
2. **Superelasticity (Pseudoelasticity):** Up to 8% recoverable strain (vs. <0.5% for SS) — perfect for self-expanding stents and orthodontic wires.

The **transformation temperature (Af, Austenite finish)** can be tuned between –20°C and +60°C by adjusting Ni/Ti ratio.

$$\text{Ms} \;\; \longrightarrow \;\; \text{Mf} \;\; \longleftrightarrow \;\; \text{As} \;\; \longrightarrow \;\; \text{Af}$$
(Martensite start, finish ↔ Austenite start, finish)

### 2.5.2 Magnesium (Mg) Alloys — The Biodegradable Frontier

- Density: **1.74 g/cm³** (closest to bone: 1.85 g/cm³)
- Young's Modulus: **~45 GPa** (matches bone perfectly)
- **Biodegradable** in vivo (corrosion product is Mg²⁺, a naturally occurring ion)
- Limitation: **Too fast corrosion** → hydrogen gas pocket formation; current research focuses on alloying with Al, Zn, Mn, rare earths, and surface coatings

### 2.5.3 Tantalum (Ta) & Zirconium (Zr)

- **Tantalum**: Excellent biocompatibility, used for sutures, stents, and as porous "trabecular metal" (Trabecular Metal™ by Zimmer) for bone ingrowth.
- **Zirconium**: Forms ZrO₂, used as alloying element in next-gen β-Ti alloys and as ceramic (Y-TZP) in dental crowns.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Comparative Matrix

### 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Essential)

| # | Equation / Metric | Formula | Units | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PREN (Pitting Resistance)** | $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3\cdot\%\text{Mo} + 16\cdot\%\text{N}$ | dimensionless | Ranking alloy corrosion resistance |
| 2 | **Stress Shielding Ratio** | $R_{ss} = \dfrac{E_{\text{implant}}}{E_{\text{bone}}}$ | ratio | Lower = better |
| 3 | **Fatigue Strength Fraction** | $\dfrac{\sigma_f}{\sigma_{\text{UTS}}}$ | ratio | Typically 0.4–0.5 for SS, 0.5–0.6 for Ti |
| 4 | **Density Ratio** | $\dfrac{\rho_{\text{implant}}}{\rho_{\text{bone}}}$ | ratio | Ti ≈ 2.4×, SS ≈ 4.3×, Mg ≈ 0.94× |
| 5 | **Strain (Hooke's Law)** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}$ | dimensionless | Implant deflection under load |
| 6 | **Nitinol Transformation ΔT** | $\Delta T \approx k \cdot (\%\text{Ni}_{\text{deviation}})$ | °C / at% | Tune Af temperature |

### 📊 Master Comparison Table (Most-Used KTU Visual)

| Property | 316L SS | Co-Cr (F75) | CP-Ti (Gr 4) | Ti-6Al-4V | Cortical Bone |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | 8.0 | 8.3 | 4.51 | 4.43 | 1.85 |
| E (GPa) | 193 | 210 | 105 | 110 | 17 |
| σ_y (MPa) | 200 | 450 | 550 | 880 | 130–180 |
| UTS (MPa) | 550 | 700 | 680 | 950 | 70–150 |
| Elongation (%) | 40 | 8 | 15 | 14 | 1–3 |
| Biocompatibility | Moderate (Ni²⁺ release) | Good (Co²⁺ concern) | Excellent | Excellent (V²⁺ concern) | — |
| MRI Compatibility | No (ferromagnetic) | No | **Yes** | **Yes** | Yes |
| Cost | **Low** | High | Medium | Medium | — |
| Main Use | Temporary fixation | Joint replacements | Dental implants | **Bone plates, stems** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Ti is MRI-compatible but 316L is not:** 316L is **austenitic** (FCC) but may contain traces of ferrite or martensite after cold working → mildly ferromagnetic → causes image artefacts. Co-Cr is non-magnetic but can heat up in MRI fields. **CP-Ti and Ti-6Al-4V are fully non-magnetic** and safe for MRI — a major clinical advantage.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN) Ranking

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

**Given** the compositions of three candidate stainless steels, determine which is most resistant to pitting corrosion in a chloride-rich physiological environment.

| Alloy | Cr (%) | Mo (%) | N (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** (304 SS) | 18.0 | 0.0 | 0.05 |
| **B** (316 SS) | 17.0 | 2.5 | 0.05 |
| **C** (316L + 2.5Mo) | 19.5 | 3.5 | 0.10 |

### 3.1.2 Governing Equation

The **PREN equation** is an empirical relationship derived from electrochemical studies of stainless steels in chloride solutions:

$$\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$$

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1: Compute PREN for Alloy A (304 SS).**
Substitute the values into the equation:
$$\text{PREN}_A = 18.0 + 3.3 \times 0.0 + 16 \times 0.05$$
Evaluate the Mo term:
$$3.3 \times 0.0 = 0.0$$
Evaluate the N term:
$$16 \times 0.05 = 0.80$$
Add the contributions:
$$\text{PREN}_A = 18.0 + 0.0 + 0.80 = 18.80$$

**Step 2: Compute PREN for Alloy B (316 SS).**
Substitute the values:
$$\text{PREN}_B = 17.0 + 3.3 \times 2.5 + 16 \times 0.05$$
Evaluate the Mo term:
$$3.3 \times 2.5 = 8.25$$
Evaluate the N term:
$$16 \times 0.05 = 0.80$$
Add the contributions:
$$\text{PREN}_B = 17.0 + 8.25 + 0.80 = 26.05$$

**Step 3: Compute PREN for Alloy C (316L + 2.5Mo).**
Substitute the values:
$$\text{PREN}_C = 19.5 + 3.3 \times 3.5 + 16 \times 0.10$$
Evaluate the Mo term:
$$3.3 \times 3.5 = 11.55$$
Evaluate the N term:
$$16 \times 0.10 = 1.60$$
Add the contributions:
$$\text{PREN}_C = 19.5 + 11.55 + 1.60 = 32.65$$

### 3.1.4 Comparative Analysis

| Alloy | PREN | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| A (304 SS) | **18.80** | Unsuitable — below 22 threshold for surgical use |
| B (316 SS) | **26.05** | Acceptable — standard implant grade |
| C (316L + 2.5Mo) | **32.65** | **Best** — high pitting resistance |

> **Conclusion:** Higher Cr, Mo, and N raise PREN → higher pitting resistance in Cl⁻ environments. Mo contributes **3.3×** as effectively as Cr per wt%, making it the most potent alloying addition for surgical stainless steels.

### 3.1.5 Engineering Validation

The empirical constants in PREN (1 for Cr, 3.3 for Mo, 16 for N) reflect the **relative effectiveness of each element in stabilising the passive Cr₂O₃ layer against chloride attack**, as determined experimentally by Lorenz & Medawar (1969) and refined by subsequent electrochemical studies. The PREN is now a standard acceptance criterion in **ASTM F138** for surgical stainless steel.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stress Shielding Quantification

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A 12 mm diameter **femoral stem** is implanted in a femur. Compute the **load carried by the bone** vs. the **load carried by the implant** for two materials, assuming **iso-strain** composite action (both bone and implant deform equally).

**Given:**
- $E_{\text{bone}} = 17$ GPa
- $E_{\text{316L}} = 193$ GPa
- $E_{\text{Ti-6Al-4V}} = 110$ GPa
- $A_{\text{implant}} = 113$ mm² (π × 6²)
- $A_{\text{bone (effective)}} = 200$ mm² (cortical ring)

### 3.2.2 Governing Principle — Iso-Strain Load Sharing

Under iso-strain ($\varepsilon_{\text{implant}} = \varepsilon_{\text{bone}} = \varepsilon$):
$$\sigma_i = E_i \cdot \varepsilon \quad ; \quad F_i = \sigma_i \cdot A_i = E_i \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A_i$$

Load share for material $i$:
$$\frac{F_i}{F_{\text{total}}} = \frac{E_i A_i}{E_{\text{implant}} A_{\text{implant}} + E_{\text{bone}} A_{\text{bone}}}$$

### 3.2.3 Step-by-Step Calculation for 316L

**Step 1: Compute $E_i A_i$ for each component.**
For implant (316L):
$$E_i A_i = 193 \times 113 = 21{,}809 \text{ GPa·mm}^2$$
For bone:
$$E_b A_b = 17 \times 200 = 3{,}400 \text{ GPa·mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Sum the stiffness contributions.**
$$E_{\text{total}} A_{\text{total}} = 21{,}809 + 3{,}400 = 25{,}209 \text{ GPa·mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute the load share for the implant.**
$$\frac{F_{\text{316L}}}{F_{\text{total}}} = \frac{21{,}809}{25{,}209} = 0.865 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 86.5\%$$

**Step 4: Compute the load share for the bone.**
$$\frac{F_{\text{bone}}}{F_{\text{total}}} = 1 - 0.865 = 0.135 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 13.5\%$$

### 3.2.4 Step-by-Step Calculation for Ti-6Al-4V

**Step 1: Compute $E_i A_i$ for the implant.**
$$E_i A_i = 110 \times 113 = 12{,}430 \text{ GPa·mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Sum stiffness contributions.**
$$E_{\text{total}} A_{\text{total}} = 12{,}430 + 3{,}400 = 15{,}830 \text{ GPa·mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute the load share for the implant.**
$$\frac{F_{\text{Ti}}}{F_{\text{total}}} = \frac{12{,}430}{15{,}830} = 0.785 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 78.5\%$$

**Step 4: Compute the load share for the bone.**
$$\frac{F_{\text{bone}}}{F_{\text{total}}} = 1 - 0.785 = 0.215 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 21.5\%$$

### 3.2.5 Comparative Conclusion

| Material | Implant Load Share | Bone Load Share | Clinical Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| **316L** | 86.5% | 13.5% | **Severe stress shielding** → bone resorption |
| **Ti-6Al-4V** | 78.5% | 21.5% | Moderate shielding — clinically accepted |

**Key Insight:** The bone load share **increases by ~59%** when switching from 316L to Ti-6Al-4V, even though the modulus difference between Ti and SS is only ~83 GPa. This demonstrates why Ti alloys are preferred for **cementless long-term implants** in younger, active patients.

### 3.2.6 Hypothetical β-Ti (E = 60 GPa) — The Future

**Step 1: Compute $E_i A_i$ for hypothetical β-Ti.**
$$E_i A_i = 60 \times 113 = 6{,}780 \text{ GPa·mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Sum stiffness contributions.**
$$E_{\text{total}} A_{\text{total}} = 6{,}780 + 3{,}400 = 10{,}180 \text{ GPa·mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute the load share for the bone.**
$$\frac{F_{\text{bone}}}{F_{\text{total}}} = \frac{3{,}400}{10{,}180} = 0.334 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 33.4\%$$

> **Conclusion:** Low-modulus β-Ti alloys (E = 55–80 GPa) would deliver **~33% of the physiological load to the bone**, more than double the Ti-6Al-4V value. This is the engineering motivation for next-generation implant alloy research.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Nitinol Transformation Temperature Tuning

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

For a self-expanding **cardiovascular stent**, the Austenite Finish temperature (Af) must be **slightly below body temperature (37°C)** so that the stent is fully deployed once warmed by blood. Determine the required Ni content given the empirical relation:
$$A_f(\,^{\circ}\text{C}) = 240 - 1100 \cdot x_{\text{Ni excess}}$$

where $x_{\text{Ni excess}}$ is the **excess Ni content in atomic fraction above 50%**.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Set the target Af for in-body deployment.**

The stent should complete its transformation at ~32°C (5°C below body temperature to ensure full deployment). Therefore:
$$A_f = 32\,^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the empirical equation.**

$$32 = 240 - 1100 \cdot x_{\text{Ni excess}}$$

**Step 3: Rearrange to isolate $x_{\text{Ni excess}}$.**

$$1100 \cdot x_{\text{Ni excess}} = 240 - 32 = 208$$
$$x_{\text{Ni excess}} = \frac{208}{1100} = 0.1891$$

**Step 4: Convert to atomic percent of Ni required.**

Total Ni content (at%):
$$x_{\text{Ni}} = 0.50 + 0.1891 = 0.6891 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 68.91\,\text{at\% Ni}$$

**Step 5: Validate.**

Check by substituting back:
$$A_f = 240 - 1100 \cdot (0.1891) = 240 - 208 = 32\,^{\circ}\text{C} \;\checkmark$$

> **Conclusion:** The stent alloy should contain **~69 at% Ni, 31 at% Ti**, slightly Ni-rich compared to the equiatomic composition, to ensure complete superelastic deployment at body temperature.

---

| Lowest modulus for bone-implant | Ti alloys (especially β-Ti, Ti-6Al-4V) |

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mechanism of Osseointegration on Ti

This block diagram maps the **sequence of bio-physico-chemical events** that occur at the Ti implant ↔ bone interface (a high-yield KTU topic).

```mermaid
    D --> E[Step 5: Calcium and phosphate ions attach to -OH]

    G --> H[Step 8: Osteoblast cells colonise HA layer]
    H --> I[Step 9: Osteoblasts secrete collagen and bone matrix]
    I --> J[Step 10: Mineralised bone forms direct bond OSSEOINTEGRATION]

    subgraph A_SURFACE[At the TiO2 surface]
        D
        E
        F
    end

    subgraph B_CELL[At the cellular level]
        H
        I
        J
    end
```

## 4.3 Architecture — Passivation and Pitting of 316L in Physiological Cl⁻

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[316L Implant exposed to 0.9% NaCl at 37C] --> B[Spontaneous formation of Cr2O3 passive layer 2 to 5 nm]
    B --> C{Passive layer intact?}
    C -- YES --> D[Implant protected corrosion rate less than 0.01 mm per year]
    C -- NO scratched, Cl- attack --> E[Local breakdown of Cr2O3]
    E --> F[Anodic dissolution of Fe2+ and Cr3+]
    F --> G[Pit nucleation and growth]
    G --> H[Stress concentration at pit tip]
    H --> I[Fatigue crack initiation]
    I --> J[Implant failure]

    B --> K{Mo content greater than 2 percent}
    K --> L[Forms MoO4 2- at pit base]
    L --> M[Repassivation of pit]
    M --> N[Pit growth arrested]
```

> **Pedagogical Purpose:** This diagram visually links the **PREN formula** (Section 3.1) to the **physical corrosion mechanism**. The Mo branch shows *why* Mo is added at 2–3% — it actively repassivates pits, a property called "molybdate self-healing."

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Explain why low-carbon grade "316L" stainless steel is preferred over standard 316 for surgical implants. (3 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

1. **(1 mark)** The "L" denotes **Low carbon content (≤ 0.03 wt%)**. Standard 316 has ~0.08% C.
2. **(1 mark)** When heated to 450–850°C (e.g., during welding or stress relief), the higher C in standard 316 reacts with Cr to form **chromium carbide (Cr₂₃C₆)** at grain boundaries — a phenomenon called **"sensitisation."**
3. **(1 mark)** This depletes Cr from the grain boundary zone, breaking down the **Cr₂O₃ passive layer** locally and causing **intergranular corrosion** in the chloride-rich physiological environment — a catastrophic failure mode. The 0.03% C limit in 316L prevents carbide formation, preserving the passive layer's integrity.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**What is the role of Molybdenum (Mo) in 316L stainless steel implants? (3 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

1. **(1 mark)** Mo is added at **2–4 wt%** in 316L primarily to enhance **pitting corrosion resistance** in chloride-containing environments (like body fluids).
2. **(1 mark)** Quantitatively, Mo contributes **3.3 times** more effectively than Cr per wt% in the **Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN)** formula: $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3\cdot\%\text{Mo} + 16\cdot\%\text{N}$.
3. **(1 mark)** Mechanistically, Mo **repassivates** pits by forming **molybdate ions (MoO₄²⁻)** at the pit base, arresting further dissolution — this is the so-called "**molybdate self-healing effect**" that makes 316L clinically viable for ~2 years of implantation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and contains two sub-parts of **7 marks each** (typically (a) and (b)). Students answer **either OR both alternatives** as per the choice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the composition, properties, and biomedical applications of **316L stainless steel** as a metallic biomaterial. Why is it the most widely used temporary implant material despite the availability of titanium and Co-Cr alloys? (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the concept of **shape memory effect and superelasticity** in **Ni-Ti (Nitinol) alloys**. Discuss its biomedical applications and the underlying thermo-mechanical phase transformation mechanism. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a):

**1. Composition of 316L (ASTM F138) — (2 marks)**

| Element | wt% | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Fe | Balance | Matrix |
| Cr | 17–20 | Passive Cr₂O₃ layer |
| Ni | 12–14 | Austenite (FCC) stabiliser |
| Mo | 2–4 | Pitting resistance |
| C | ≤ 0.03 | Low C → prevents sensitisation |
| Mn, Si, P, S | Traces | Processing aids |

**2. Key Properties — (2 marks)**

- Yield strength: ~200 MPa (annealed), ~700 MPa (cold-worked)
- UTS: ~550 MPa
- Young's modulus: 193 GPa
- Elongation: 40–50% (excellent ductility)
- Density: 8.0 g/cm³
- PREN: ~25–27

**3. Biomedical Applications — (1 mark)**

- Bone plates, screws, intramedullary nails
- Temporary fracture fixation devices
- Surgical instruments
- Hip stems (historical, now mostly replaced)

**4. Why Widely Used Despite Alternatives — (2 marks)**

- **Lowest cost** among all implant metals (5–10× cheaper than Ti/Co-Cr)
- **Excellent formability and weldability** for custom implants
- **Adequate biocompatibility** for short-term (≤ 2 years) use
- **Well-established manufacturing standards** (ASTM F138/F139)
- **High ductility** allows drawing into wires and thin sheets
- When implant is to be **removed** after healing (e.g., bone plates), 316L is the optimal cost-performance choice

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b):

**1. Two Unique Phenomena in Ni-Ti — (2 marks)**

- **Shape Memory Effect (SME):** A plastically deformed specimen **recovers its original shape upon heating** above a critical temperature (Austenite finish, Af).
- **Superelasticity:** Up to **8% fully recoverable strain** at constant temperature (above Af) — orders of magnitude higher than conventional elastic limit (~0.5%).

**2. Underlying Phase Transformation — (3 marks)**

The mechanism is a **diffusionless, thermoelastic martensitic transformation** between:
- **Austenite (parent) phase** — B2 ordered cubic, stable at high T
- **Martensite (product) phase** — B19′ monoclinic, stable at low T

Upon cooling from austenite:
$$\text{B2 (Austenite)} \xrightarrow{\text{Ms, Mf}} \text{B19' (Martensite)}$$
Upon heating:
$$\text{B19' (Martensite)} \xrightarrow{\text{As, Af}} \text{B2 (Austenite)}$$

In **SME**: The martensite is "detwinned" by applied stress, and on heating above Af, the lattice reverts to the original austenite orientation → macroscopic shape recovery.

In **Superelasticity**: Above Af, applied stress induces martensite (stress-induced martensite, SIM), and on unloading, it reverts to austenite → large recoverable strain.

**3. Biomedical Applications — (2 marks)**

- **Self-expanding cardiovascular stents** (e.g., Memotherm™, SMART™)
- **Orthodontic archwires** (provides constant force over large deflection)
- **Endodontic files** (Ni-Ti rotary instruments)
- **Spinal vertebra straighteners** (Scoliosis correction)
- **Suture anchors, bone staples**
- **Guide wires for catheterisation**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a suitable composition table, compare and contrast **CP-Titanium and Ti-6Al-4V alloy** for orthopaedic implant applications. Highlight the role of alloying elements Al and V. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the composition, classification, and clinical advantages/disadvantages of **dental metallic materials** including noble alloys, base metal alloys, and amalgams. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a):

**1. Composition Comparison — (2 marks)**

| Element / Property | CP-Ti (Grade 4) | Ti-6Al-4V (ASTM F136) |
|---|---|---|
| Titanium (Ti) | ~99% (≥ 98.9%) | ~90% |
| Aluminium (Al) | — | 5.5–6.5% |
| Vanadium (V) | — | 3.5–4.5% |
| Oxygen (O) max | 0.40% | 0.20% |
| Iron (Fe) max | 0.30% | 0.30% |
| Nitrogen (N) max | 0.05% | 0.05% |
| Hydrogen (H) max | 0.015% | 0.015% |
| Microstructure | α-phase (HCP) | α + β dual phase |
| Yield strength | ~550 MPa | ~880 MPa |
| UTS | ~680 MPa | ~950 MPa |
| E (GPa) | 105 | 110 |

**2. Role of Al — (1 mark)**

Al is an **α-phase stabiliser**. It raises the β-transus temperature, stabilising the HCP α-phase at room temperature. It also:
- Increases tensile strength (solid solution strengthening)
- Reduces density
- Concerns: long-term Al³⁺ release has been linked (debated) to **Alzheimer-like neurological effects**

**3. Role of V — (1 mark)**

V is a **β-phase stabiliser**. It lowers the β-transus temperature, retaining some BCC β-phase at room temperature. Benefits:
- Improves room-temperature ductility and formability
- Enables heat treatment strengthening (solution + aging)
- Concerns: **V⁵⁺ ions are cytotoxic** → modern implants (especially dental) trend toward **V-free alloys** like **Ti-6Al-7Nb** or **Ti-13Nb-13Zr**

**4. Clinical Application Split — (2 marks)**

- **CP-Ti (Gr 2, 4):** Dental implants, porous coatings, plates where high fatigue isn't required
- **Ti-6Al-4V:** Femoral stems, bone plates, spinal cages, trauma screws — high-load, high-fatigue applications

**5. Common Advantages — (1 mark)**

- Excellent biocompatibility (TiO₂ passive layer)
- Lowest density among major implant metals
- MRI-compatible
- High specific strength (strength/density)
- Superior osseointegration compared to SS/Co-Cr

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b):

**1. Classification of Dental Metals — (3 marks)**

| Class | Composition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **High Noble Alloys** | Au (≥ 60%) + Pd/Pt | Crowns, bridges, inlays |
| **Noble Alloys** | Pd-Ag, Pd-Cu (≥ 25% noble) | Crowns, partial dentures |
| **Base Metal Alloys** | Ni-Cr, Co-Cr (≤ 25% noble) | PFM substructure, RPD frameworks |
| **Amalgam** | Ag-Sn-Cu + Hg (mixed at chairside) | Direct posterior fillings |
| **Endodontic / Implant** | CP-Ti, Ti-6Al-4V, Ni-Ti | Dental implants, rotary files |

**2. Noble Alloys — Advantages & Disadvantages — (1.5 marks)**

**Advantages:** Excellent corrosion resistance, precise marginal fit, biocompatible, easy to cast and polish.
**Disadvantages:** Very expensive (gold price volatility), low yield strength (~200–400 MPa), high density.

**3. Base Metal Alloys (Ni-Cr, Co-Cr) — (1.5 marks)**

**Advantages:** Cheap, high strength (yield ~600 MPa), low thermal conductivity, suitable for PFM.
**Disadvantages:** **Ni²⁺ ion release causes contact dermatitis / allergic reactions** in 10–20% of population → Bi and Cr ion release also a concern. Difficult to cast and finish.

**4. Dental Amalgam — (1 mark)**

- Composition: 40–55% Ag, 25–30% Sn, up to 30% Cu, 1–2% Zn, mixed with ~50% Hg
- Forms γ (Ag₃Sn), γ₁ (Ag₂Hg₃), γ₂ (Sn₈Hg) phases
- **Advantages:** Cheap, easy to place, durable (10–30 years), self-sealing
- **Disadvantages:** Mercury toxicity concerns, poor aesthetics (silver-grey colour), non-bonded (no adhesion to tooth), declining use due to "mercury-free dentistry" trend

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU 2024 EXAMINER PITFALLS — WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Nitinol" with "Titanium"** — Nitinol is a **Ni-Ti** alloy (~55% Ni), not pure titanium. Always state both elements explicitly. (−1 to −2 marks)
>
> 2. **Writing "316" instead of "316L"** — For implants, the **L grade is mandatory**. Mark deduction if the low-carbon specification is not mentioned. (−1 mark)
>
> 3. **Forgetting the passive oxide layer mechanism** — Any corrosion resistance question without mentioning the **Cr₂O₃ (or TiO₂) passive layer** is incomplete. (−2 marks on 7-mark questions)
>
> 4. **Wrong modulus ranking** — Always remember: **Mg (~45) < β-Ti (~60) < Ti-6Al-4V (~110) < 316L (~193) < Co-Cr (~210) < Bulk metallic glass (lower)**. Memorise this once. (−2 marks)
>
> 5. **Ignoring MRI compatibility** — For any "permanent implant" question, **always** mention MRI safety of Ti-based alloys as a clinical advantage. (−1 mark)
>
> 6. **Conflating "noble" and "precious" metals** — In dental alloys, **Ag is precious but NOT noble**. Use ADA's classification correctly. (−1 mark)
>
> 7. **Skipping the role of Mo in pitting resistance** — When discussing 316L, the 3.3× Mo contribution in PREN is a guaranteed 1–2 mark "decoration" point.
>
> 8. **Writing "cobalt causes cancer"** — Be precise. Co²⁺ ions are **cytotoxic at high concentrations**, but Co-Cr alloys have decades of safe clinical use. Avoid sensational statements.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

Use this **high-density checklist** for last-minute revision:

- ✅ **Metallic biomaterials** = bio-inert load-bearers; not bioactive (no bone bonding without surface treatment).

- ✅ **Three key evaluation criteria:** Biocompatibility, mechanical performance, corrosion resistance.

- ✅ **316L SS** is **austenitic (FCC)**, low carbon (≤ 0.03%), 17–20% Cr, 12–14% Ni, 2–4% Mo. Used for **temporary** implants. PREN ≈ 25.

- ✅ **Co-Cr alloys** (F75 cast, F562 wrought) have the **highest wear resistance** and strength. Used in **femoral heads** of hip joints. E ≈ 210 GPa.

- ✅ **CP-Ti (Grades 1–4)** strength increases with oxygen content. Grade 4 (O = 0.4%) is the strongest CP-Ti.

- ✅ **Ti-6Al-4V** is α+β alloy; **Al stabilises α (HCP)**, **V stabilises β (BCC)**. The workhorse of orthopaedic implants.

- ✅ **Ti alloys are MRI-safe** (non-magnetic) → major advantage over 316L and Co-Cr.

- ✅ **β-Ti alloys** (Ti-13Nb-13Zr, Ti-35Nb-5Ta-7Zr) have **low modulus (~55–80 GPa)** → reduced stress shielding.

- ✅ **Stress shielding** arises because implant E >> bone E (17 GPa). Bone resorbs per Wolff's Law.

- ✅ **Nitinol (Ni-Ti)** = shape memory + superelasticity. Used in **stents, orthodontic wires, endodontic files**.

- ✅ **Dental metals** classified by ADA: **High noble (≥60% noble, ≥40% Au), Noble (≥25% noble), Base metal (<25% noble).**

- ✅ **Amalgam** is Ag-Sn-Cu + Hg; **high-Cu amalgam** (>20% Cu) eliminates weak γ₂ phase.

- ✅ **Mg alloys** are **biodegradable** (E ~ 45 GPa matches bone) but corrode too fast — research material.

- ✅ **Passive oxide layer** ranks: **TiO₂ (most stable) > Cr₂O₃ > Fe oxides** (least stable).

- ✅ **PREN formula:** $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3\cdot\%\text{Mo} + 16\cdot\%\text{N}$. **Higher = better pitting resistance.**

- ✅ **Modulus ranking (low to high):** Bone (17) < Mg (45) < β-Ti (60) < CP-Ti (105) ≈ Ti-6Al-4V (110) < 316L (193) < Co-Cr (210).

- ✅ **Density ranking (low to high):** Mg (1.74) < Ti (4.5) < 316L (8.0) < Co-Cr (8.3).

- ✅ **MRI safety ranking (safe to unsafe):** Ti & Mg > Co-Cr > 316L SS.

- ✅ **Always state the standard** for each alloy in exam answers (e.g., "316L — ASTM F138", "Ti-6Al-4V — ASTM F136", "Co-Cr-Mo — ASTM F75").

> **🎯 KTU 2024 Final Tip:** When a question asks "which metal for X application?", structure your answer in three blocks: **(1) Mechanical property required → (2) Corrosion/biocompatibility requirement → (3) Cost and MRI constraint.** This is the exact KTU valuation key structure that fetches full 7 marks per sub-part.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT414 - BIOMATERIALS & APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Corrosion of Metallic Implants - Manufacturing Implants - Mechanical Properties - Applications in Medicine - Biocompatibility - Host and Tissue Response - In-Vitro and In-Vivo Tests for Implants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1: Fundamentals of Metallic Biomaterials and Biological Response

## 1. Corrosion of Metallic Implants

### Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrosion** is defined as the irreversible physico-chemical interaction between a metal (or alloy) and its surrounding physiological environment, resulting in the **deterioration of the metal's bulk properties**, the **release of metal ions into the tissue**, and the **loss of structural integrity** of the implant. In the human body, this occurs in an aggressive, **oxygenated, warm (~37 °C), chloride-rich (~0.9 % NaCl), protein-laden aqueous electrolyte**.

The body is essentially a **warm saline bath** with a pH of **7.35–7.45**, which accelerates the thermodynamic tendency of metals to revert to their native oxidized ore form. This makes corrosion the **single most critical failure mode** of load-bearing metallic implants such as hip stems, fracture plates, and dental fixtures.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine leaving an iron nail outside in the rain. Within weeks, it rusts (red-brown Fe₂O₃ flakes) and eventually crumbles. Now imagine placing the same nail **inside a warm, salty, aerated aquarium** (which chemically mimics blood plasma) — it would rust **ten to one hundred times faster**.

An orthopaedic implant lives inside an even more aggressive version of that aquarium:

- **Salinity of blood plasma** ≈ 9 g/L NaCl → accelerates galvanic and pitting attack.
- **Temperature 37 °C** → reaction kinetics roughly double every 10 °C (Arrhenius behaviour).
- **Dynamic mechanical loading** → causes fretting and wear-accelerated corrosion.
- **Dissolved O₂, proteins, amino acids, and cells** → change the electrochemical surface chemistry continuously.

> [!NOTE]
> A metallic implant in the body is therefore NOT in a "safe" environment — it is in a *corrosion-testing laboratory* 24 hours a day. The designer's job is to slow this attack to clinically negligible levels.

---

## 2. Manufacturing of Metallic Implants

### Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implant manufacturing** encompasses the full set of metallurgical, forming, machining, finishing, and surface-treatment processes used to convert raw medical-grade alloys (Ti-6Al-4V, 316L SS, Co-Cr-Mo) into finished implants with the required **geometry, surface finish, mechanical strength, and corrosion resistance**.

The manufacturing route directly influences **grain size, residual stress, surface oxide layer, fatigue life, and biocompatibility** — a poorly finished implant can fail in months, while a properly finished one can last 20+ years.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of manufacturing as the difference between a **hand-forged kitchen knife** and a **surgical scalpel**. Both are made of steel, but the scalpel is:

1. Cast or vacuum-arc remelted to remove inclusions.
2. Forged/hot-rolled to break the dendritic cast structure.
3. Machined to micron-level tolerances.
4. Polished, passivated, and cleaned to a mirror finish.
5. Sterilized in a cleanroom.

A bone screw or hip stem is made the same way — at industrial scale and under ISO 13485 / ASTM F standards.

> [!NOTE]
> Manufacturing determines the **microstructure** (α/β phases in Ti alloys, grain size in 316L SS) which in turn dictates every mechanical and corrosion property downstream.

---

## 3. Mechanical Properties of Metallic Implants

### Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanical properties** are the quantitative descriptors of a material's response to applied forces — including **elastic modulus (E), yield strength (σ_y), ultimate tensile strength (σ_UTS), elongation (%), fatigue strength (S-N curve), hardness (HV), fracture toughness (K_IC), and wear resistance**. For implants, these properties must be matched as closely as possible to those of the host bone to avoid **stress shielding, micromotion, and aseptic loosening**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine inserting a **rigid steel pipe** into a soft wooden beam. When you load the beam, the steel carries almost all the load and the wood "sees" very little stress → it weakens and atrophies. This is **stress shielding**, and it is the #1 cause of long-term bone loss around stiff implants.

The ideal implant is **stiff enough to bear load, yet flexible enough to share load with bone**. This is why the **apparent elastic modulus** of an implant is a critical design parameter:

- Cortical bone: **E ≈ 10–20 GPa**
- Ti-6Al-4V: **E ≈ 110 GPa** (5–10× bone)
- Co-Cr-Mo: **E ≈ 210 GPa** (10–20× bone)
- 316L SS: **E ≈ 190 GPa**

> [!NOTE]
> Modern research focuses on **porous and lattice-structured implants** that reduce effective E to match bone (~10–20 GPa) without losing strength.

---

## 4. Applications of Metallic Implants in Medicine

### Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Metallic implants are used in nearly every surgical specialty for **load-bearing structural support, fixation, articulation, and electrical stimulation**. The three principal alloy families — **316L stainless steel, Co-Cr-Mo, and Ti-based alloys** — dominate the clinical landscape for orthopaedic, dental, cardiovascular, and neurological applications.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Modern medicine would be unimaginable without metals:

- **Broken bone** → titanium plate + screws (fixation).
- **Worn-out hip joint** → Co-Cr-Mo ball + Ti stem (arthroplasty).
- **Missing tooth** → Ti dental implant screw (osseointegration).
- **Blocked artery** → 316L SS or Co-Cr stent (vascular scaffold).
- **Spinal fracture** → Ti pedicle screws + rods (spinal fusion).
- **Cochlear damage** → Pt-Ir electrodes (neural stimulation).

> [!NOTE]
> The selection of alloy for a given application is governed by the **Pugh Matrix** of: (1) biocompatibility, (2) mechanical demand, (3) corrosion environment, (4) cost, and (5) MRI compatibility.

---

## 5. Biocompatibility

### Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biocompatibility** is the ability of a material to perform its **desired function** in the body **without eliciting any unacceptable local or systemic adverse effect**, while simultaneously inducing an **appropriate host response** for that specific application (Williams' Definition, 2008 — adopted by the FDA and KTU 2024 syllabus). It is a **function of the material AND the host environment**, not a fixed material property.

A material that is biocompatible for a **bone plate** may be toxic as a **cardiovascular stent**, because the host tissue and the consequences of failure are different.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of biocompatibility like **real-estate zoning**: a factory (a toxic material) is fine in an industrial zone (subcutaneous tissue with low reactivity) but disastrous in a residential zone (brain or heart). The same chemical composition can be:

- **Biocompatible** in bone (Ti hip stem).
- **Tolerated** in muscle (316L suture wire).
- **Toxic** in the brain (heavy-metal ion release causes neuronal death).

> [!NOTE]
> Biocompatibility testing in KTU 2024 syllabus is divided into **three tiers**: (a) ISO 10993 cytotoxicity, (b) sensitization/irritation, (c) systemic, sub-chronic, and chronic toxicity, genotoxicity, and implantation studies.

---

## 6. Host and Tissue Response

### Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Host response** is the **cascade of biological events** initiated when a foreign material is introduced into living tissue, including **injury, blood–material interaction, provisional matrix formation, acute inflammation, chronic inflammation, granulation tissue formation, foreign body reaction, and finally fibrosis/fibrous capsule formation** (Anderson's classical sequence, adopted by KTU 2024). The response can be **biomechanically neutral, beneficial (osseointegration), or destructive (chronic inflammation, osteolysis, metal sensitivity)**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you step on a **thorn** that embeds in your foot:

1. **0–24 h**: Redness, swelling, pain → acute inflammation.
2. **1–3 days**: White blood cells attack the thorn.
3. **3–7 days**: Macrophages try to engulf the thorn (foreign body reaction).
4. **>7 days**: If the thorn cannot be digested, the body walls it off in a fibrous capsule (fibrous encapsulation).
5. **If the thorn keeps shedding debris** (e.g., a corroding metal implant) → chronic inflammation continues indefinitely.

An implant is the body's equivalent of a thorn that **cannot be removed** and continuously leaches ions, wear debris, and corrosion products.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Foreign Body Giant Cell (FBGC)** is the histological signature of chronic implant-induced inflammation and is a key marker in KTU in-vivo biocompatibility testing.

---

## 7. In-Vitro and In-Vivo Tests for Implants

### Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In-vitro tests** (Latin: "in glass") are laboratory experiments performed on isolated cells, tissues, or biomolecules in controlled environments (petri dishes, bioreactors). **In-vivo tests** (Latin: "in the living") are experiments conducted in living animal models (rats, rabbits, dogs, sheep, pigs) under approved ethics protocols. Together, they form the **biocompatibility evaluation pyramid** mandated by ISO 10993 and the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of testing an implant like testing a new **food additive**:

- **In-vitro** = lab analysis of chemical reaction with stomach fluid in a beaker (fast, cheap, but limited).
- **In-vivo** = feeding it to a rat for 90 days and observing organ histology (slow, expensive, but realistic).

You cannot ship the additive without both. Likewise, no implant is clinically approved without **both in-vitro cytotoxicity AND in-vivo animal implantation data**.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 exam frequently tests the **distinction between in-vitro and in-vivo tests**, the **sequence of testing (cytotoxicity → sensitization → implantation)**, and the **ISO 10993 standard numbers** for each test.

---

## 8. Summary of Key Terminology

| Term | One-Line Definition | Unit / Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Corrosion** | Irreversible electrochemical degradation of a metal in physiological electrolyte. | mpy or mm/year |
| **Implant** | A medical device placed inside the body for ≥ 30 days. | — |
| **Biocompatibility** | Ability to function without unacceptable host response. | Qualitative |
| **Stress Shielding** | Bone resorption due to stiff implant carrying physiological load. | — |
| **Host Response** | Biological cascade from injury to fibrous encapsulation. | — |
| **In-vitro** | Test in controlled cell/tissue culture outside a living organism. | — |
| **In-vivo** | Test in a living animal model. | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Corrosion of Metallic Implants — Electrochemical Mechanism

### 1.1 The Electrochemical Cell

A corroding metal in physiological fluid behaves as a **short-circuited galvanic cell** with four essential components:

1. **Anode** → metal dissolution (oxidation) site: $M \rightarrow M^{n+} + n e^-$
2. **Cathode** → reduction site: most commonly $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$
3. **Electrolyte** → physiological saline (≈ 0.9 % NaCl, ionically conductive).
4. **Electrical path** → the metal itself (electrons flow anode → cathode through the implant).

> [!NOTE]
> The **corrosion current (I_corr)** measured in **µA/cm²** is the *rate metric* of corrosion. A high I_corr = short implant life. ASTM F2129 sets the standard for cyclic polarization testing of small implant devices.

### 1.2 Types of Corrosion Relevant to Implants

- **Uniform (General) Corrosion** → even material loss across the surface; rare in modern alloys due to passive films.
- **Pitting Corrosion** → localized attack through passive film breakdown, especially in Cl⁻-rich environments (Ti, 316L SS).
- **Crevice Corrosion** → occurs in tight gaps (modular taper junctions in hip implants).
- **Galvanic Corrosion** → when two dissimilar metals are coupled (e.g., SS screw + Ti plate).
- **Fretting Corrosion** → combined mechanical wear + chemical attack at micromotion interfaces.
- **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** → crack propagation under tensile stress + corrosive environment.
- **Corrosion Fatigue** → cyclic loading + corrosive environment = accelerated fatigue failure.

### 1.3 Pourbaix Diagrams and the Passive Film

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **passive oxide film** (e.g., TiO₂ on Ti, Cr₂O₃ on Co-Cr alloys) is what gives modern implant alloys their remarkable corrosion resistance. It is **only a few nanometers thick** but self-healing in oxidizing environments, provided the local pH stays between roughly **2 and 12**.

**Pourbaix Diagram Concept**: A plot of electrode potential (E) vs. pH showing regions of immunity, corrosion, and passivation. For a Ti implant at body pH 7.4, the stable phase is **TiO₂ (passive)** — exactly what we want.

### 1.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Corrosion

| Parameter | Formula / Definition | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Corrosion Rate (CR) | $CR = \frac{K \cdot I_{corr} \cdot EW}{\rho}$ | mm/year | $K = 0.00327$ (constant), $EW$ = equivalent weight, $\rho$ = density |
| Faraday's Law | $m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | grams | $F = 96{,}485$ **C/mol** |
| Tafel Equation | $\eta = \pm \beta \log_{10}\left(\frac{i}{i_0}\right)$ | V | $\beta$ = Tafel slope, $i_0$ = exchange current density |
| Nernst Equation | $E = E^\circ - \frac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | V | Predicts equilibrium potential |
| Breakdown Potential (E_b) | Potential at which pitting initiates | V vs. SCE | Higher E_b ⇒ more pitting-resistant |
| Polarization Resistance (R_p) | $R_p = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta i}$ at $i = 0$ | $\Omega \cdot cm^2$ | Inversely proportional to I_corr |
| Stern–Geary | $I_{corr} = \frac{\beta_a \beta_c}{2.303 R_p (\beta_a + \beta_c)}$ | A/cm² | Used to extract I_corr from linear polarization |
| Passive Current Density (i_pass) | Current density in passive region | µA/cm² | Lower = better passivity |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU exams**, the most-tested formulas are **Faraday's Law**, **Tafel equation**, and the **Stern–Geary relation**. Numerical problems almost always revolve around computing **corrosion rate in mm/year** from a given I_corr.

### 1.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Corrosion science underpins:

- **ASTM F2129** — Standard Test Method for Conducting Cyclic Potentiodynamic Polarization to Determine the Corrosion Susceptibility of Small Implant Devices.
- **ASTM F746** — Pitting/Crevice Corrosion of Metallic Surgical Implant Materials.
- **Selection of alloy for spinal rods, dental abutments, and pacemaker cans**.
- **Lifetime prediction** of an implant from in-vitro electrochemical data + Arrhenius extrapolation.

---

## 2. Manufacturing of Metallic Implants

### 2.1 Process Route (Block Diagram in §4)

**Typical manufacturing chain for a Ti-6Al-4V hip stem:**

1. **Primary Melting** → Vacuum Arc Remelting (VAR) or Vacuum Induction Melting (VIM) to remove O₂, N₂, H₂ inclusions.
2. **Secondary Melting** → Cold Hearth / Electron Beam melting for further purity.
3. **Forming** → Hot forging, hot rolling, or extrusion to break dendritic structure.
4. **Machining** → CNC turning/milling to final geometry (5-axis for complex shapes).
5. **Surface Treatment** → passivation (nitric acid for SS), anodization (for Ti), polishing.
6. **Cleaning** → ultrasonic + deionized water rinse, ISO Class 7 cleanroom.
7. **Sterilization** → gamma irradiation (preferred for polymers) or autoclave (for metals).
8. **Packaging** → double-blister, vacuum-sealed, sterile barrier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Investment casting** ("lost-wax") is used for complex Co-Cr-Mo orthopaedic implants (knee femoral components) where machining is uneconomical.

### 2.2 Surface Modification Techniques

| Technique | Effect on Surface | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Polishing** | Reduces Ra roughness from µm to nm | All implants — improves fatigue + corrosion |
| **Electropolishing** | Anodic dissolution of surface asperities | 316L SS, Co-Cr — final finish |
| **Passivation (HNO₃)** | Thickens Cr₂O₃ / TiO₂ layer | ASTM F86 standard |
| **Anodization** | Controlled TiO₂ thickness (blue/gold/colour) | Dental abutments, identification |
| **Plasma Spraying (HA)** | Hydroxyapatite coating for osseointegration | Cementless hip stems |
| **Sandblasting / Grit-blasting** | Macro-roughness for bone on-growth | Acetabular cups |
| **Acid Etching** | Micro-roughness | Dental implants (SLA surface) |
| **PVD / DLC coatings** | Diamond-Like Carbon — wear + barrier | Co-Cr joint surfaces |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **ASTM F136** — Wrought Ti-6Al-4V ELI (Extra Low Interstitial) for surgical implants.
- **ASTM F75 / F799** — Co-Cr-Mo casting and wrought standards.
- **ASTM F138 / F139** — 316L stainless steel bar and wire for surgical implants.
- **ISO 5832** series — International equivalents.

---

## 3. Mechanical Properties — Detailed Theory

### 3.1 Stress–Strain Curve Anatomy

For a typical ductile implant alloy under uniaxial tension:

- **Region I — Linear Elastic**: $σ = E \cdot \varepsilon$ (Hooke's Law).
- **Region II — Yielding**: plastic deformation begins at $\sigma_y$ (0.2 % offset method).
- **Region III — Strain Hardening**: $\sigma$ increases to $\sigma_{UTS}$.
- **Region IV — Necking**: local cross-section reduction.
- **Region V — Fracture**: at strain $\varepsilon_f$.

### 3.2 Key Mechanical Property Table

| Property | Symbol | 316L SS | Co-Cr-Mo | Ti-6Al-4V | Cortical Bone |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Elastic Modulus | E (GPa) | **190** | **210–250** | **110** | **10–20** |
| Yield Strength | σ_y (MPa) | **690** | **450–1000** | **795** | **100–150** |
| UTS | σ_UTS (MPa) | **860** | **900–1300** | **860** | **50–150** |
| Elongation | % | **40** | **8–20** | **10–15** | **1–5** |
| Fatigue Strength (10⁷ cycles) | σ_f (MPa) | **300** | **500** | **450** | **—** |
| Density | ρ (g/cm³) | **7.9** | **8.3** | **4.5** | **1.8–2.0** |
| Hardness | HV | **150–200** | **300–400** | **320–380** | **—** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Density matters for implants!** A hip stem made of Ti-6Al-4V weighs ~50 % less than a 316L SS or Co-Cr equivalent — critical for patient comfort in large implants.

### 3.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Mechanical Properties

| Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Hooke's Law | $σ = E \cdot ε$ | Linear elastic region |
| Stress | $σ = F/A_0$ | MPa (N/mm²) |
| Strain | $ε = \Delta L / L_0$ | Dimensionless or % |
| Poisson's Ratio | $ν = -\varepsilon_{lateral}/\varepsilon_{axial}$ | ≈ 0.3 for most metals |
| Shear Modulus | $G = E / [2(1+ν)]$ | GPa |
| Bulk Modulus | $K = E / [3(1-2ν)]$ | GPa |
| Stress Shielding Factor | $SSF = 1 - (E_{implant}/E_{bone})$ ... conceptual | Lower E_implant ⇒ less shielding |
| Endurance Limit | $σ_e \approx 0.5 \cdot σ_{UTS}$ (for steels) | Empirical; Ti has no true endurance limit |
| Basquin's Law | $σ_a = σ_f' (2N_f)^b$ | High-cycle fatigue |
| Coffin–Manson | $\varepsilon_a = \varepsilon_f' (2N_f)^c$ | Low-cycle fatigue |
| Goodman | $σ_a / σ_{UTS} + σ_m / σ_{UTS} = 1$ | Mean stress correction |
| Soderberg | $σ_a / S_e + σ_m / σ_y = 1$ | Conservative fatigue criterion |
| Hertzian Contact Stress | $σ_{max} = \frac{3F}{2\pi a^2}$ | Bearing surfaces (hip joint) |
| Wear Volume (Archard) | $V = k \cdot F \cdot s / H$ | $k$ = wear coefficient, $H$ = hardness |

### 3.4 Real-World Utility

- **Design of a femoral stem** uses a **factor of safety of 2–3** on fatigue to ensure 20-year survival.
- **Modulus mismatch** is addressed by **porous coatings, functionally graded materials, and composite stems**.
- **Wear debris** from Co-Cr heads on UHMWPE acetabular cups causes **osteolysis** — leading cause of revision surgery.

---

## 4. Applications in Medicine — Categorization

### 4.1 Orthopaedic & Trauma

- Bone plates, screws, intramedullary nails (316L SS, Ti).
- Total hip arthroplasty (THA): Co-Cr head + Ti stem + UHMWPE cup.
- Total knee arthroplasty (TKA): Co-Cr femoral component + Ti tibial baseplate.
- Spinal cages, pedicle screws, rods (Ti-6Al-4V, Ti-Nb-Zr for MRI compatibility).

### 4.2 Dental

- Endosseous implants (commercially pure Ti grade 4).
- Orthodontic wires (Ni-Ti, Ti-Mo).
- Crowns and bridges (Co-Cr, Au-Pd).

### 4.3 Cardiovascular

- Stents (316L SS, Co-Cr L605, Pt-Cr, Mg alloys for bioresorbable).
- Heart valve frames (Co-Cr, Ti).
- Pacemaker cans (Ti).
- Vena cava filters (Ni-Ti shape memory alloy).

### 4.4 Neurological & ENT

- Cochlear electrode arrays (Pt-Ir).
- Cranial plates and meshes (Ti).
- CSF shunts (silicone + Ti connectors).

### 4.5 Other

- Surgical instruments (martensitic 440 SS, sometimes Ti).
- Sutures and staples (Ti, 316L).
- Tissue engineering scaffolds (porous Ti, Mg alloys).

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection of alloy by application (KTU-favorite table):**

| Application | Preferred Alloy | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Temporary fixation (bone plates, screws) | **316L SS** | Low cost, adequate strength, easy removal |
| Permanent load-bearing (hip stem) | **Ti-6Al-4V** | Low modulus, biocompatible, MRI-compatible |
| Articulating surfaces (hip head, knee) | **Co-Cr-Mo** | High wear resistance, high strength |
| Dental implant | **CP Ti (Grade 4)** | Best osseointegration |
| Cardiovascular stent | **316L SS, Co-Cr L605, Pt-Cr** | Thin struts, high radial strength |
| Bioresorbable (research) | **Mg-Zn-Ca alloys** | Degrades in 6–24 months |

---

## 5. Biocompatibility — Hierarchical Testing Concept

### 5.1 Definitions and Levels

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Biocompatibility is **application-specific**, not a single yes/no property. A material may be biocompatible for a bone plate (osseointegration desired) and incompatible for an intraocular lens (transparency + chemical inertness required).

### 5.2 Biocompatibility Testing Pyramid (ISO 10993)

- **Tier 1 — Cytotoxicity (ISO 10993-5)**: in-vitro cell culture (e.g., L929 mouse fibroblasts). Tests extract, direct contact, indirect contact. Endpoints: cell viability ≥ 70 %.
- **Tier 2 — Sensitization & Irritation (ISO 10993-10)**: guinea pig maximization test (GPMT), local lymph node assay (LLNA), intracutaneous reactivity.
- **Tier 3 — Systemic Toxicity (ISO 10993-11)**: acute, sub-acute, sub-chronic, chronic in rodents.
- **Tier 4 — Genotoxicity (ISO 10993-3)**: Ames test, mouse lymphoma, micronucleus.
- **Tier 5 — Implantation (ISO 10993-6)**: in-vivo in bone, muscle, subcutaneous tissue; histology at 1, 4, 12, 26 weeks.
- **Tier 6 — Special Studies**: carcinogenicity, reproductive toxicity, biodegradation.

### 5.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Biocompatibility

| Concept | Definition | Test / Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Cell Viability (%) | $\% \text{ viability} = \frac{OD_{sample}}{OD_{control}} \times 100$ | MTT / XTT assay, ISO 10993-5 |
| Extract Dilution | Usually 0.1 g material / 1 mL vehicle | ISO 10993-12 |
| Stimulation Index (SI) | $SI = \frac{cpm_{test}}{cpm_{control}}$ | LLNA, ISO 10993-10 |
| Sensitization Threshold | SI ≥ 3 ⇒ positive sensitizer | OECD 429 |
| Histological Score | Semi-quantitative 0–4 scale | ISO 10993-6 |
| Osseointegration % | BIC = bone-to-implant contact | Histomorphometry |

---

## 6. Host and Tissue Response — Anderson's Cascade

### 6.1 The Classical Sequence (1977 → 2008 Update)

| Phase | Time | Key Event | Cells / Mediators |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Injury | 0 | Surgical incision | Tissue damage, blood–implant contact |
| 2. Provisional Matrix | seconds–minutes | Protein adsorption (fibrinogen, albumin, IgG, complement) | Vroman effect |
| 3. Acute Inflammation | min–days | Neutrophil infiltration | PMNs, IL-1, TNF-α |
| 4. Chronic Inflammation | days–weeks | Monocyte → macrophage infiltration | Macrophages, IL-6, MCP-1 |
| 5. Foreign Body Reaction | weeks | Macrophage fusion → FBGC | Multinucleated giant cells |
| 6. Granulation Tissue | 1–2 weeks | Neovascularization, fibroblast infiltration | VEGF, TGF-β, fibroblasts |
| 7. Fibrous Encapsulation | 2–4 weeks | Collagen capsule formation | Type I/III collagen, FBGC |
| 8. Resolution or Chronicity | months | Stable fibrous capsule OR chronic inflammation | Depends on ion release, wear debris |

### 6.2 Effect of Implant Properties on Host Response

| Implant Property | Effect on Host |
|---|---|
| **Surface chemistry** (TiO₂ vs. CoCr) | Modulates protein adsorption → cell adhesion |
| **Surface roughness (Ra)** | Ra < 0.5 µm → fibrous encapsulation; Ra > 1 µm → bone on-growth |
| **Surface energy / wettability** | High surface energy → better cell adhesion |
| **Porosity** | Pore 100–400 µm → bone ingrowth; < 5 µm → fibrous tissue only |
| **Ion release** | Ni, Co, Cr, V → cytotoxic, allergenic |
| **Wear debris** | Sub-micron particles → macrophage activation, osteolysis |
| **Mechanical mismatch** | Stress shielding → bone resorption |

### 6.3 KTU Formulas / Quantitative Measures

- **Foreign Body Ratio (FBR)**: area of FBGC / total area at implant–tissue interface (histology).
- **Capsule Thickness (µm)**: average fibrous capsule thickness around implant.
- **Inflammatory Cell Density**: cells / mm² at peri-implant zone.

---

## 7. In-Vitro vs In-Vivo Tests — Comparative Matrix

| Aspect | In-Vitro | In-Vivo |
|---|---|---|
| **System** | Isolated cells / tissues | Whole living animal |
| **Throughput** | High (96-well plates, automated) | Low (one animal per time point) |
| **Cost** | Low | High (housing, ethics, personnel) |
| **Time** | Hours to days | Weeks to years |
| **Data type** | Mechanistic, molecular, cellular | Systemic, histological, functional |
| **Standards** | ISO 10993-3, -5 | ISO 10993-6, -11, OECD GLP |
| **Examples** | MTT, Ames, hemolysis, platelet adhesion | Subcutaneous implant, bone implantation, muscle implantation |
| **Advantages** | Reproducible, controlled, no ethical issues | Realistic, captures systemic response |
| **Disadvantages** | No systemic context, simplified | Ethics, inter-subject variability, expensive |

### 7.1 Detailed List of Standard Tests

#### A) In-Vitro Tests

1. **Cytotoxicity (ISO 10993-5)** — MEM Elution, Agar Diffusion, Direct Contact. End-point: cell viability.
2. **Genotoxicity (ISO 10993-3)** — Ames test, mouse lymphoma assay, chromosomal aberration, micronucleus.
3. **Hemocompatibility (ISO 10993-4)** — hemolysis, thrombogenicity, complement activation, platelet aggregation.
4. **Sensitization (ISO 10993-10)** — LLNA (in-vitro alternatives now accepted in some jurisdictions).
5. **Irritation (ISO 10993-23)** — Reconstructed Human Epidermis (RhE) test, e.g., EpiDerm SIT.
6. **Material Characterization (ISO 10993-18)** — chemical composition, surface analysis (XPS, SEM-EDS, AFM).

#### B) In-Vivo Tests

1. **Acute Systemic Toxicity (ISO 10993-11)** — single dose in mouse/rat, observe 14 days.
2. **Subacute / Subchronic Toxicity (ISO 10993-11)** — repeated dose 28–90 days.
3. **Implantation Tests (ISO 10993-6)**:
   - **Subcutaneous** (rat dorsal) — short-term soft-tissue response.
   - **Intramuscular** (rabbit paravertebral) — long-term soft tissue.
   - **Intraosseous / Bone** (rabbit tibia, dog femur) — osseointegration.
4. **Sensitization (ISO 10993-10)** — GPMT in guinea pig (in-vivo).
5. **Carcinogenicity (ISO 10993-13 / OECD 451)** — 2-year rodent bioassay.
6. **Reproductive & Developmental Toxicity (ISO 10993-16)**.

### 7.2 Decision Flow for KTU

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the testing sequence:** **"C S I G S R"** → Cytotoxicity → Sensitization → Irritation → Genotoxicity → Systemic → Reproductive. This is the order in which tests are typically conducted in the ISO 10993 evaluation.

---

## 8. Cross-Cutting KTU High-Yield Points

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Module 1 (KTU 2024):** the most-tested cross-connections are:
> 1. **Corrosion ↔ Biocompatibility**: ion release causes host response.
> 2. **Mechanical Properties ↔ Stress Shielding**: modulus mismatch causes bone resorption.
> 3. **Manufacturing ↔ Microstructure ↔ Properties**: process → grain size → strength, fatigue, corrosion.
> 4. **Surface finish ↔ Host response**: roughness controls cell fate (fibroblast vs. osteoblast).
> 5. **In-vitro screening before in-vivo**: ISO 10993 tiered logic.
