# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Define Porosity
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

**Q:** Define porosity of a biomaterial. How is it experimentally measured using the Archimedes principle?

**Model Answer:**
> **Porosity** ($\varepsilon$) is defined as the ratio of the volume of pores ($V_P$) to the total volume ($V_T$) of the biomaterial, expressed as a fraction or percentage.
> Mathematically: $\varepsilon = V_P / V_T$.
>
> **Archimedes Method (3-step procedure):**
> 1. Measure **dry weight** $W_{\text{dry}}$ of the scaffold.
> 2. Measure **saturated weight** $W_{\text{sat}}$ after vacuum-impregnation in water.
> 3. Measure **suspended weight** $W_{\text{sus}}$ when fully immersed in water.
>
> Porosity is then computed as:
> $\varepsilon = \dfrac{W_{\text{sat}} - W_{\text{dry}}}{W_{\text{sat}} - W_{\text{sus}}} \times 100\%$

### Question 2 — Pore Size Selection
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**Q:** Why is a pore size of 100–500 µm preferred for bone tissue engineering scaffolds?

**Model Answer:**
> Pores in the range **100–500 µm** are recommended for bone scaffolds because:
> - They permit **osteoblast migration** into the scaffold interior.
> - They allow **vascularization** (blood vessel in-growth) which is critical for cell survival in thick constructs.
> - They facilitate **mass transport** of oxygen, nutrients, and metabolic waste.
> - Pores **< 100 µm** lead to surface-only cell attachment with risk of **hypoxia** in the core.
> - Pores **> 500 µm** reduce available surface area for cell attachment and lower mechanical strength.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — Fabrication & Characterization of Porous Scaffolds

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the **solvent casting and particulate leaching** method for fabricating porous polymeric scaffolds. Discuss the role of porogen (e.g., NaCl) particle size in determining final pore size. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the various **techniques used to characterize porous biomaterials** (porosity, pore size, morphology, and mechanical strength). Mention at least four methods with their working principles. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

> **Step 1 — Principle**: Solvent casting + particulate leaching is a *conventional* scaffold fabrication method based on dissolving a polymer in an organic solvent, mixing it with a water-soluble porogen (salt/sugar), and then leaching out the porogen with water to leave behind pores.

> **Step 2 — Procedure**:
> 1. **Dissolve** the polymer (e.g., PLGA, PCL) in an organic solvent (e.g., chloroform, dichloromethane) to form a viscous solution.
> 2. **Add** porogen particles (NaCl, sucrose, gelatin) of desired size — typically sieved to **100–500 µm** for bone scaffolds.
> 3. **Cast** the polymer–porogen mixture into a mold and **evaporate** the solvent (room temperature or vacuum oven).
> 4. **Leach** the porogen by immersing in deionized water for 24–48 hours with multiple water changes.
> 5. **Dry** the scaffold under vacuum to obtain a porous structure.

> **Step 3 — Role of Porogen Size**:
> The **final pore size** of the scaffold is directly controlled by the **size of the porogen particles**:
> - **Small porogens** (e.g., 50 µm NaCl) → small pores (microporous)
> - **Large porogens** (e.g., 500 µm NaCl) → large pores (macroporous)
> - The **porogen-to-polymer weight ratio** controls porosity; typical ratios range from **4:1 to 9:1** giving 70–90% porosity.

> **Step 4 — Advantages**: Simple, inexpensive, controllable pore size.
> **Step 5 — Limitations**: Use of toxic organic solvents; long leaching times; only thin scaffolds (< 3 mm) can be made; residual solvent can harm cells.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

> **Four Characterization Techniques**:

| Method | Property Measured | Working Principle |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Mercury Intrusion Porosimetry (MIP)** | Pore size distribution, total porosity | Mercury is forced into pores under high pressure; volume intruded vs. pressure gives pore size via Washburn equation |
| **Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)** | Pore morphology, surface topography | High-resolution electron beam scans scaffold surface; gives 2D image of pore shape and interconnectivity |
| **Micro-Computed Tomography (µ-CT)** | 3D pore architecture, porosity | X-rays scan scaffold slice-by-slice; reconstructed into 3D image; software calculates porosity and pore size |
| **Uniaxial Compression Testing** | Compressive strength, modulus | Scaffold placed between platens; load applied at constant rate; stress–strain curve yields $\sigma$ and $E$ |
| **Gas Adsorption (BET)** | Specific surface area | N₂ gas adsorbed at 77 K; BET equation gives SSA from monolayer coverage |
| **Archimedes Method** | Bulk porosity, density | Liquid displacement technique — gives $\varepsilon$ and $\rho_{\text{bulk}}$ |

> **Conclusion**: No single method gives complete information; a **combination of techniques** (e.g., SEM + MIP + µ-CT + mechanical testing) is required for comprehensive porous scaffold characterization.

---

### QUESTION B — Permeability & Kozeny–Carman Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the **Kozeny–Carman equation** for permeability of a porous scaffold. State clearly the assumptions and significance of each term. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A porous PLGA scaffold used for cartilage tissue engineering has a porosity of **75%** and an effective pore diameter of **120 µm**. If water ($\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) is forced through the scaffold under a pressure gradient of **10 kPa across 4 mm thickness**, calculate the **volumetric flow rate per unit area** (i.e., the Darcy flux). Assume $C_K = 5$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

> **Assumption 1**: The porous medium can be modeled as a *bundle of tortuous, non-uniform capillaries*.

> **Assumption 2**: Flow within each capillary obeys **Hagen–Poiseuille's law**:
> $Q = \dfrac{\pi r_c^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$

> **Step 1**: Introduce the **hydraulic radius**:
> $r_h = \dfrac{\text{void volume}}{\text{wetted surface}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{S_{\text{spec}}(1-\varepsilon)}$

> **Step 2**: Replace capillary radius with $r_h$ in the Poiseuille relation, and account for **tortuosity** $\tau$ (path lengthening due to winding channels) and **shape factor** $C_K$ (Kozeny constant).

> **Step 3**: After empirical calibration, the **Kozeny–Carman equation** emerges:
> $\boxed{\;k = \dfrac{\varepsilon^{3}}{C_K (1-\varepsilon)^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}}{180}\;}$

> **Significance of each term**:
> - $\varepsilon^{3}$ — Permeability is *highly sensitive* to porosity (a small change in $\varepsilon$ causes a large change in $k$).
> - $(1-\varepsilon)^{2}$ in denominator — High porosity is necessary for adequate permeability.
> - $d^{2}$ — Larger pores dramatically increase permeability (square dependence).
> - $C_K$ — Accounts for pore geometry: $C_K \approx 5$ for fibrous scaffolds, $C_K \approx 2$ for granular media.
> - $180$ — Empirical constant derived for spherical particles; varies for irregular pores.

> **Coupling with Darcy's Law**:
> $Q = \dfrac{k A \Delta P}{\mu L} = \dfrac{A \Delta P}{\mu L} \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon^{3} d^{2}}{180\, C_K (1-\varepsilon)^{2}}$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

> **Given Data**:
> - $\varepsilon = 0.75$
> - $d = 120\ \mu\text{m} = 120 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$
> - $\Delta P = 10\ \text{kPa} = 10{,}000\ \text{Pa}$
> - $L = 4\ \text{mm} = 4 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$
> - $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$
> - $C_K = 5$

> **Step 1 — Calculate Permeability**:
> - $\varepsilon^{3} = (0.75)^{3} = 0.4219$
> - $(1-\varepsilon)^{2} = (0.25)^{2} = 0.0625$
> - $d^{2} = (120 \times 10^{-6})^{2} = 1.44 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m}^{2}$

$$
k = \frac{0.4219}{5 \times 0.0625} \cdot \frac{1.44 \times 10^{-8}}{180}
$$

$$
k = \frac{0.4219}{0.3125} \cdot 8.0 \times 10^{-11}
$$

$$
k = 1.350 \cdot 8.0 \times 10^{-11}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;k = 1.08 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}^{2} = 108\ \text{nm}^{2}\;}
$$

> **Step 2 — Calculate Darcy Flux** $q = Q/A$:

$$
q = \frac{k \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L} = \frac{(1.08 \times 10^{-10})(10{,}000)}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3})(4 \times 10^{-3})}
$$

$$
q = \frac{1.08 \times 10^{-6}}{4.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.270\ \text{m/s}
$$

> **Final Answer**: $\boxed{\;q \approx 0.27\ \text{m/s}\;}$ (or **27 cm/s**)

> **Verification**: This is a physiologically relevant perfusion rate, comparable to capillary blood flow velocities (~0.3–0.5 cm/s in capillaries), confirming the PLGA scaffold is suitable for nutrient transport in cartilage tissue engineering.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks in Porous Materials Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing porosity with pore size** — Porosity is a *volume fraction* (dimensionless), pore size is a *length* (µm). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these are used interchangeably.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in Kozeny–Carman calculations** — Permeability $k$ must be reported in **m²** (SI). Many students incorrectly report it in cm² or mm², losing 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Mixing up $\rho_{\text{bulk}}$ and $\rho_{\text{skeletal}}$** — $\rho_{\text{bulk}} < \rho_{\text{skeletal}}$ always. Reversing the ratio gives porosity $> 1$ (physically impossible).
>
> 4. **Using $(1+\varepsilon)$ instead of $(1-\varepsilon)$ in KC equation** — This is a frequent typo. Recheck.
>
> 5. **Missing the *connectivity* aspect in answers** — For tissue engineering, **interconnected pores** (not just porous) are required. Mentioning "interconnectivity" earns bonus marks.
>
> 6. **Not stating assumptions of KC equation** — Examiners expect students to mention *bundle of capillaries model*, *Poiseuille flow*, and *tortuosity*.
>
> 7. **Gibson–Ashby exponent confusion** — For modulus: exponent is **2**; for strength: exponent is **3/2**. Mixing these is a common error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Bullet Revision — Porous Materials**

- **Porosity ($\varepsilon$)** = void volume / total volume; ranges from **0 (dense) to 1 (fully void)**. Measured by Archimedes method.

- **Three pore size classes** (IUPAC): **Micro** (< 2 nm), **Meso** (2–50 nm), **Macro** (> 50 nm). Bone scaffolds require **macro-pores 100–500 µm**.

- **Open vs. Closed pores**: Open (interconnected) pores are essential for tissue in-growth; closed pores are biologically useless.

- **Specific Surface Area (SSA)** = $6\varepsilon / d$ for spherical pores. Smaller pore size → higher SSA → better for drug delivery.

- **Kozeny–Carman equation**:
  $k = \dfrac{\varepsilon^{3}}{C_K(1-\varepsilon)^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}}{180}$ — coupled with **Darcy's law** to find flow.

- **Darcy's Law**: $Q = (k A \Delta P) / (\mu L)$ — the *fundamental* transport equation in porous media.

- **Gibson–Ashby Model**: Modulus scales as $(1-\varepsilon)^{2}$, strength as $(1-\varepsilon)^{3/2}$ for cellular solids.

- **Strength–Porosity trade-off**: Higher porosity → lower strength. Balanced design is critical (typically **60–80% porosity** for bone scaffolds).

- **Pore size for bone**: **> 100 µm** minimum; **200–500 µm** optimal for vascularization.

- **Fabrication methods**: Conventional (solvent casting, particulate leaching, gas foaming, freeze drying) vs. Advanced (3D printing, electrospinning, biomimetic self-assembly).

- **Characterization tools**: SEM (morphology), MIP (pore size distribution), µ-CT (3D architecture), BET (SSA), Archimedes (porosity), compression testing (mechanical).

- **Key biomaterials for porous scaffolds**: **Ceramics** (HA, β-TCP, bioactive glass), **Polymers** (PLA, PLGA, PCL, collagen, chitosan), **Metals** (porous Ti, Ta), **Composites**.

- **Clinical applications**: Bone tissue engineering, drug delivery carriers, wound dressings, dental implants, vascular grafts.

- **Mechanical units reminder**: Strength in **MPa**, modulus in **GPa**, permeability in **m²** (or Darrcy = 9.87 × 10⁻¹³ m²).

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 — POROUS MATERIALS NOTES**

# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Biocompatibility of Composites — Formal KTU Definition

**Biocompatibility of a composite** is defined as the *ability of a multi-phase material — composed of a biocompatible matrix phase and a biocompatible reinforcement (or filler) phase — to perform its intended function in a physiological environment while eliciting an appropriate host response, without producing any adverse local or systemic effects.* It is a **system-level property**, not a property of a single phase, and is governed by the synergistic interaction of:

1. **Matrix biocompatibility** (e.g., PLA, PEEK, PHB, chitosan)
2. **Filler/Fiber biocompatibility** (e.g., HA, bioglass, cellulose, silk fibroin)
3. **Interfacial biocompatibility** (the matrix–fiber boundary — the *third phase*)
4. **Processing-induced surface chemistry** (residual monomers, crystallinity, surface energy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Biocompatibility of composites is evaluated as per **ISO 10993** series of standards. The composite must be tested **as a finished, sterilized device** — not as individual constituents — because the interface can leach degradation products that neither phase releases alone.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Tendon and Bone" Analogy)

Imagine your **tendon connecting to bone**. Nature has solved the composite problem: collagen fibers (the *reinforcement*) transfer load through a graded interface into hydroxyapatite mineral (the *matrix*). A **biocomposite for medical use is the engineer’s attempt to mimic this graded, multi-phase biological architecture**.

A second analogy: think of a biocomposite as a **"cooperative bus"**:
- The **matrix** is the *bus body* — carries passengers, determines surface interaction with tissue.
- The **fibers** are the *chassis* — provide mechanical strength.
- The **interface** is the *axle connecting body to chassis* — if it fails, the whole system fails *even though both parts are individually good*.

---

## 1.3 Production Techniques for Biocomposite Parts — Overview

KTU 2024 classifies biocomposite processing into two families based on matrix nature:

| Family | Matrix Type | Examples | Typical Technique |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermoplastic biocomposites** | Melt-processable bio-polymers | PLA, PEEK, PHBV, PCL | **Extrusion, Injection Molding** |
| **Thermoset biocomposites** | Reactive resins | Epoxy + bio-fibers, unsaturated polyester + natural fibers | **Filament Winding, Hand Lay-up, RTM** |

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of processing technique directly dictates the **residual stress state, fiber orientation, porosity, and surface chemistry** of the final implant — all of which govern its in-vivo biocompatibility.

---

## 1.4 Conventional Composite Processing Techniques — Classification

For completeness, KTU expects familiarity with the **four classical families**:

1. **Open-mold processes** — Hand lay-up, spray-up (low cost, high porosity).
2. **Closed-mold processes** — Compression molding, RTM, VARTM.
3. **Continuous processes** — **Filament winding**, pultrusion.
4. **Bulk/Discontinuous processes** — **Extrusion, injection molding, blow molding** (for short-fiber thermoplastics).

This module focuses on **Extrusion, Injection Molding, and Filament Winding** as the *board-weightage* techniques.

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Thermophysical Reasoning Behind Biocomposite Processing

### 2.1.1 Why Processing Affects Biocompatibility
Every processing step imposes a **thermal, shear, and chemical history** on the biocomposite:

- **Shear heating** → can degrade biopolymers (e.g., PLA molecular weight loss above **190 °C**).
- **Residual stress** → dictates fatigue life of load-bearing implants.
- **Surface crystallinity** → controls protein adsorption and cell adhesion.
- **Fiber attrition (breakage)** → reduces mechanical reinforcement efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Why is twin-screw extrusion preferred over single-screw for biocomposites?"*
> **Answer:** Twin-screws provide *better distributive and dispersive mixing* of bio-fibers (which are typically shorter, more fragile, and moisture-sensitive than glass/carbon fibers) at *lower residence times*, minimizing thermal degradation.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### 2.2.1 Single-Screw Extrusion (Drag-Dominated Flow)

The screw extruder channel can be modeled as a *shallow rectangular channel* un-wrapped from the screw. The flow consists of three superposed components.

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_d = \dfrac{\pi^2 \, D^2 \, N \, H \, \sin\theta\cos\theta}{2}$ | **Drag flow** (forward, due to barrel rotation) | m³/s |
| 2 | $Q_p = -\dfrac{W \, H^3 \, \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$ | **Pressure flow** (back-flow, die resistance) | m³/s |
| 3 | $Q = Q_d + Q_p - Q_l$ | **Net output** (after leakage $Q_l$ over flights) | m³/s |
| 4 | $\dot{\gamma}_{wall} = \dfrac{\pi D N}{H}$ | **Apparent shear rate** at barrel wall | s⁻¹ |
| 5 | $T_{melt} = T_{barrel} + \dfrac{\eta \, \dot{\gamma}^2}{k \, \rho c_p}$ | **Melt temperature rise** from viscous dissipation | K |

*Where:* $D$ = screw diameter, $N$ = screw rpm, $H$ = channel depth, $\theta$ = helix angle, $W$ = channel width, $\mu$ = melt viscosity, $L$ = down-channel length, $\eta$ = non-Newtonian viscosity.

### 2.2.2 Injection Molding

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 6 | $F_{clamp} = P_{cav} \cdot A_{proj}$ | **Clamping tonnage** to resist cavity pressure | N |
| 7 | $\Delta P_{runner} = \dfrac{12 \mu Q L}{w \, h^3}$ | **Rectangular runner pressure drop** (for $h \ll w$) | Pa |
| 8 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{h^2 \, \rho \, c_p}{\pi^2 k} \ln\!\left[\dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_{eject} - T_w}\right]$ | **Cooling time** in mold cavity | s |
| 9 | $V_{shot} = V_{cavity} \,(1 + \alpha_{shrink})$ | **Total shot volume** with shrinkage compensation | m³ |

*Where:* $P_{cav}$ = cavity pressure (~ **35–80 MPa**), $A_{proj}$ = projected area, $h$ = part thickness, $T_w$ = mold wall temperature, $T_{eject}$ = ejection temperature, $k$ = thermal conductivity.

### 2.2.3 Filament Winding

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{v_{t}}{\pi D \, N_{m}} = \dfrac{L_{rev}}{\pi D}$ | **Winding angle** (fiber to axis) | rad/° |
| 11 | $w_{b} = \dfrac{\pi \, d_{f}}{\sin\alpha}$ | **Band width** on mandrel surface | m |
| 12 | $n_{b} = \dfrac{\pi D}{w_{b}\,\cos\alpha}$ | **Number of bands** in a single circuit | – |
| 13 | $T_{fiber} = T_0 \cdot e^{\mu_{f} \phi}$ | **Tension buildup** over $\phi$ wrap angle with friction $\mu_f$ | N |

*Where:* $v_t$ = traverse (carriage) speed, $N_m$ = mandrel rotational speed, $L_{rev}$ = axial advance per revolution, $d_f$ = fiber tow width, $D$ = mandrel diameter.

### 2.2.4 Biocompatibility Test Thresholds (KTU Memory Aid)

| ISO Test | Common Method | Time | Pass Criterion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cytotoxicity (ISO 10993-5) | MTT / MEM Elution | 24–72 h | Cell viability $\geq$ **70 %** |
| Sensitization (ISO 10993-10) | Guinea-pig maximization | 24–48 h | Grade 0 or 1 (no erythema) |
| Irritation (ISO 10993-23) | Intracutaneous | 0–72 h | Score $\leq$ **1.0** |
| Systemic toxicity (ISO 10993-11) | Acute systemic | 14 d | No mortality, no organ toxicity |

> [!NOTE]
> The literal symbol `$\vert$` (absolute value) is used in numerical cutoffs (e.g., score $\leq \vert 1.0 \vert$); render as `$\vert 1.0 \vert$` in LaTeX to avoid table-pipe conflicts.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Net Flow in a Single-Screw Extruder

We model the screw channel as a **shallow rectangular channel** of width $W$, depth $H$, and down-channel length $L$, inclined at helix angle $\theta$. The barrel moves relative to the screw at velocity $V_b = \pi D N$ at angle $\theta$ to the channel axis.

### Step 1 — Resolve barrel velocity along the channel
The barrel surface velocity has a component along the down-channel direction:

$$
V_{bz} = \pi D N \cos\theta
$$

### Step 2 — Drag flow (Couette component)
Assuming a Newtonian fluid and a no-slip boundary at the barrel wall, the velocity profile is linear in $z$ (perpendicular to barrel). The volumetric drag flow is the integral of this linear profile across the channel cross-section:

$$
Q_d = \int_{0}^{W} \int_{0}^{H} v_z(y) \, dy \, dx
$$

For a Couette flow driven by a moving wall, the velocity at any depth $y$ is:

$$
v_z(y) = V_{bz} \cdot \dfrac{y}{H}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Q_d = W \int_{0}^{H} V_{bz} \cdot \dfrac{y}{H} \, dy = W \cdot V_{bz} \cdot \dfrac{H}{2}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute channel width $W$
For a deep channel (ignoring flight wall contribution), the width is the screw circumference projected perpendicular to the flight:

$$
W = \pi D \sin\theta - H \cos\theta \;\approx\; \pi D \sin\theta
$$

### Step 4 — Combine
Inserting $V_{bz}$ and $W$ into the drag flow:

$$
Q_d = \left(\pi D \sin\theta\right) \cdot \left(\pi D N \cos\theta\right) \cdot \dfrac{H}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q_d = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2}\,}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** $Q_d$ is maximized at $\theta = 45°$ (since $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta$ peaks at $\theta = 45°$). This is why *most extrusion screws use a 17.7° to 30°* helix (compromise between high $Q_d$ and good conveying).

### Step 5 — Pressure flow (Poiseuille component)
When a die creates back-pressure $\Delta P$ along the down-channel length $L$, a parabolic counter-flow develops:

$$
Q_p = -\dfrac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}
$$

The negative sign indicates flow is *opposite* to the drag flow direction. This is the classic **Hagen–Poiseuille** result for a rectangular slit with aspect ratio $H \ll W$.

### Step 6 — Leakage flow over the flight lands
A small flow $Q_l$ leaks between the flight land and the barrel surface (a thin gap $\delta$). For Newtonian fluid and treating this gap as a narrow slit:

$$
Q_l = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 \delta^3 \tan\theta \,\Delta P}{12 \mu E}
$$

where $E$ is the flight width perpendicular to the barrel surface.

### Step 7 — Net extruder output (Operating characteristic)

$$
\boxed{\,Q = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2} \;-\; \dfrac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L} \;-\; \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 \delta^3 \tan\theta \,\Delta P}{12 \mu E}\,}
$$

This is the **screw characteristic curve** — a straight line in the $Q$–$\Delta P$ plane. When intersected with the **die characteristic** (also a straight line through the origin for Newtonian fluid), it gives the *operating point*.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Filament Winding Angle

Consider a fiber being laid on a mandrel of diameter $D$ rotating at $N_m$ rpm. The carriage (payoff eye) moves axially at velocity $v_t$. The fiber makes a **single helix** on the cylindrical surface.

### Step 1 — Distance traveled in one revolution
In one complete revolution (time $T = 1/N_m$), the carriage advances axially by:

$$
L_{rev} = v_t \cdot T = \dfrac{v_t}{N_m}
$$

### Step 2 — Circumferential distance in one revolution
The fiber travels a circumferential distance equal to the mandrel perimeter:

$$
C = \pi D
$$

### Step 3 — Tangent of winding angle
The winding angle $\alpha$ is the angle the fiber makes with the **mandrel axis** (KTU convention). From the right-triangle geometry:

$$
\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\text{axial advance per rev}}{\text{circumferential dist per rev}} = \dfrac{L_{rev}}{\pi D}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{v_t}{\pi D N_m}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{L_{rev}}{\pi D}\right)\,}
$$

### Step 4 — Band width
The fiber tow has a width $d_f$ (perpendicular to fiber axis). The projected band width $w_b$ on the mandrel surface, measured *parallel to the axis*, is:

$$
w_b = \dfrac{d_f}{\cos\alpha} \quad \text{(geometric projection onto axial direction)}
$$

However, the *band spacing* along the circumferential direction (KTU standard) is:

$$
\boxed{\,w_{b,circ} = \dfrac{\pi D}{n_b\,\cos\alpha}\,}
$$

where $n_b$ is the integer number of bands in a single helical circuit.

### Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU-style)
Given: $D = 0.1$ m, $N_m = 30$ rpm, $v_t = 0.05$ m/s, $d_f = 5$ mm.

Step (a): $L_{rev} = 0.05 / 30 = 1.667 \times 10^{-3}$ m.
Step (b): $\tan\alpha = 1.667\times10^{-3} / (\pi \times 0.1) = 5.305 \times 10^{-3}$.
Step (c): $\alpha = \arctan(5.305 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.304° \approx 0.30°$ — an *almost-circumferential* wind.
Step (d): $w_b = d_f / \cos\alpha = 0.005 / 0.99999 \approx 0.005$ m.
Step (e): $n_b = \pi D / w_b = \pi \times 0.1 / 0.005 = 62.8$ → take **62 bands** to avoid overlap (geodesic path condition).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Processing–Property–Biocompatibility Linkage

| Processing Parameter | Mechanical Effect | Surface/Chemical Effect | Biocompatibility Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Screw RPM | ↓ Molecular weight (PLA) | ↑ Shear-induced surface roughness | Faster degradation, MMT shift |
| ↑ Mold temp. ($T_w$) | ↑ Crystallinity | Smoother surface | Better cell adhesion, lower irritation |
| ↑ Winding tension | ↑ Fiber compaction | ↓ Void content | ↑ Strength, ↑ Leachables risk |
| ↑ Cooling rate | ↓ Crystallinity | ↑ Residual stress | ↑ Cracking in vivo, ↑ fatigue |
| Vacuum degassing | – | ↓ Porosity | ↓ Bacterial colonization sites |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** When asked "How does processing affect biocompatibility?", do **not** answer *only* in chemical terms. The examiner expects three pillars: **mechanical, surface, and chemical** responses.

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Biocomposite Processing Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    rawMat[RAW BIOCOMPOSITE INPUT] --> matSel{MATRIX TYPE SELECTION}

    dryStep --> extProc[EXTRUSION COMPOUNDING]
    extProc --> granStep[GRANULATION STEP]
    granStep --> injProc[INJECTION MOLDING]
    injProc --> postProc[POST PROCESSING AND STERILIZATION]
    thermoMat --> resinMix[RESIN AND HARDENER MIXING]
    resinMix --> bathStep[FIBER IMPREGNATION BATH]
    bathStep --> filWind[FILAMENT WINDING ON MANDREL]

    qcLab --> packFin[STERILE PACKAGED IMPLANT]
```

graph TD

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define biocompatibility of composites. Why is biocompatibility treated as a system property and not a property of individual phases?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Biocompatibility of composites is the ability of a multi-phase biomaterial — comprising a biocompatible matrix and biocompatible reinforcement — to perform its intended function in vivo while eliciting an appropriate host response per **ISO 10993**. It is a *system property* because the **interfacial third phase** can generate degradation products, leachables, or surface chemistries (e.g., residual catalyst at the fiber–matrix interface) that neither the matrix nor the fiber releases individually. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for ISO 10993 reference, 1 mark for interfacial argument.)

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three conventional composite processing techniques and state one biopolymer–reinforcement system best suited for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Technique | Best-suited Biopolymer | Reinforcement |
|---|---|---|
| Extrusion | PLA / PHBV | Short cellulose fibers |
| Injection Molding | PEEK / PLA | Short bioglass particles |
| Filament Winding | Epoxy (bio-based) | Continuous flax / silk fibers |

(1 mark per correct row, 0.5 mark for technique name + 0.5 mark for system.)

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Choice A — Question A

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the working principle of a **single-screw extruder** for processing thermoplastic biocomposites. Derive the **drag flow equation** and discuss how the *operating point* is obtained graphically. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 60 mm diameter single-screw extruder operates at 60 rpm. The screw has a 25° helix angle and 4 mm metering depth. If the extruder is fitted with a 6 mm long-land die, find the **net output** when the pressure at the die entry is 4 MPa. Assume melt viscosity $\mu = 350$ Pa·s and leakage gap $\delta = 0.05$ mm with flight width $E = 6$ mm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)
- **(1 mark)** Sketch: label hopper, barrel, screw, three zones (feed, compression, metering), die.
- **(2 marks)** Un-wrapping analogy: shallow rectangular channel of width $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta$, depth $H$.
- **(2 marks)** Drag flow derivation: $Q_d = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2}$.
- **(1 mark)** Pressure flow and operating point: $Q_p = -\dfrac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$; intersection with die characteristic.
- **(1 mark)** Comment on biocomposite relevance: low shear for fragile bio-fibers, vacuum venting for moisture.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $D = 0.06$ m, $N = 1$ rev/s, $\theta = 25°$, $H = 0.004$ m, $L = 0.006$ m, $\Delta P = 4 \times 10^6$ Pa, $\mu = 350$ Pa·s, $\delta = 5\times 10^{-5}$ m, $E = 0.006$ m.

**Step 1 — Drag flow:**
$$
Q_d = \dfrac{\pi^2 (0.06)^2 (1) (0.004) \sin 25° \cos 25°}{2}
$$
$$
= \dfrac{9.8696 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.4226 \times 0.9063}{2}
$$
$$
= \dfrac{9.8696 \times 1.44 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.3829}{2} = \dfrac{5.4406 \times 10^{-5}}{2}
$$
$$
\boxed{Q_d = 2.7203 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

**Step 2 — Channel width:**
$$
W = \pi D \sin\theta = \pi \times 0.06 \times \sin 25° = 0.1885 \times 0.4226 = 0.0797\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Pressure flow:**
$$
Q_p = -\dfrac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L} = -\dfrac{0.0797 \times (0.004)^3 \times 4\times 10^6}{12 \times 350 \times 0.006}
$$
$$
= -\dfrac{0.0797 \times 6.4\times 10^{-11} \times 4\times 10^6}{25.2} = -\dfrac{2.0403 \times 10^{-5}}{25.2}
$$
$$
\boxed{Q_p = -8.097 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}}
$$

**Step 4 — Leakage flow:**
$$
Q_l = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 \delta^3 \tan\theta \cdot \Delta P}{12 \mu E} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 3.6\times 10^{-3} \times (5\times 10^{-5})^3 \times 0.4663 \times 4\times 10^6}{12 \times 350 \times 0.006}
$$

Numerator: $9.8696 \times 3.6\times 10^{-3} = 0.03553$; $(5\times 10^{-5})^3 = 1.25\times 10^{-13}$; $0.03553 \times 1.25\times 10^{-13} = 4.441\times 10^{-15}$; $\times 0.4663 = 2.071\times 10^{-15}$; $\times 4\times 10^6 = 8.286 \times 10^{-9}$.

Denominator: $12 \times 350 \times 0.006 = 25.2$.

$$
Q_l = \dfrac{8.286\times 10^{-9}}{25.2} = 3.288 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Net output:**
$$
Q = Q_d + Q_p - Q_l = 2.7203\times 10^{-5} - 8.097\times 10^{-7} - 3.288\times 10^{-10}
$$
$$
\boxed{Q \approx 2.6393 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 26.4\ \text{cm}^3/\text{s}}
$$
